comments.author: Ericson2314
comments.body: IMO this is very weak motivation. It might feel good to make `std` more batteries-included in the short term, but it just leads to bloat and ossification in the long term.

comments.author: nellshamrell
comments.body: The point about the `next()` function making the `Stream` trait not object safe is a concerning one. In order to hopefully get this RFC accepted sooner than later, I'm going to take the `next()` method out for now. It's something we can do more experimentation with as an unstable part of the language and then, based on that experience, write up a new RFC for it in the future.

comments.author: MikailBag
comments.body:
I'd like to make note that, unlike `Future`, Streams will come in 4 flavors: `!Send & !Sync, Send & !Sync, !Send & Sync, Send & Sync`.

With futures, one can always wrap a future in a https://docs.rs/sync_wrapper/0.1.0/sync_wrapper/struct.SyncWrapper.html and gain Sync for free, so only two flavors are possible: `!Send & Sync, Send & Sync`.

A way to avoid such a split is to change `size_hint` to take `&mut self`.

comments.author: taiki-e
comments.body:
> to change `size_hint` to take `&mut self`.

Note that if you change to `&mut self`, you will not be able to call `size_hint` if the stream is `!Unpin` and pinned. (You need to use `self: Pin<&mut Self>` instead of `&mut self`.)

comments.author: MikailBag
comments.body:
Yeah, you're right about pinning. 

The essential here is mutability. If `size_hint` does not require a unique reference (be it &mut or Pin<&mut>), Sync is no longer available for free, which can make implementations of streams more complex.

comments.author: nellshamrell
comments.body:
Hello all!

I appreciate the additional discussion around `size_hint` and mutability. Is there anything that would potentially block acceptance of this RFC?

comments.author: tesaguri
Darksonn
comments.body: @tesaguri The point is that if you make such a `SyncWrapper`, then it cannot implement `Stream` in a way that forwards `size_hint`.

comments.author: MikailBag
tesaguri
comments.body:
Ah, I think I've got the point. So the point is that you have to fall back on the default implementation, which can be inefficient.

Thank you all for clarification!

comments.author: zesterer
comments.body:
One pain point I've consistently had with this `Stream` design in the past is that it's very difficult to determine when a stream expects to draw items (in fact, impossible).

As a quick example: the design of [`flume`](https://github.com/zesterer/flume), and indeed many other channel crates, involves registering a shibboleth 'slot' with the queue for every waiting receiver. Senders then eagerly dispatch items into these waiting shibboleth slots and wake the threads/tasks associated with them. This design (as opposed to one that involves waking receivers and them having the responsibility of polling the queue for items if they're fast enough) is significantly more performant and removing it degrades MPMC performance by something like 50% in our benchmarks, and often more in cases of very high contention.

Sadly, this doesn't work so well with futures. Until the future is dropped, it's basically impossible for the channel to determine when the future has 'lost interest' in receiving items. This isn't usually a massive problem for futures specifically: futures are usually created and awaited in-place and so we can pretty much regard them as always having interest in receiving even if this technically isn't the case (one could create a future, poll it once, and then never doing anything with it again, thereby causing an item to be lost to the ether).

(Note that this is *not* a problem for synchronous operations because we can ensure that deregister logic is executed when blocking has finished, such as in the case of `recv_timeout`)

This problem becomes even worse with streams. The reason for this is that streams may be polled for items, then left to sit for a while, before being polled for items again. This is a reason for two problems:

Firstly, because we don't know when the stream has stopped logically being polled for items, we can't implement the optimisation mentioned above without risking queue items being simply swallowed into the ether, never to be seen again.

Secondly, the fact that we do not know which streams are still actively being polled and will, to analogise, "pick up the phone when we call them" (because it's perfectly normal to leave an unused stream lying around for some time) we can't make any assumptions about streams being in a position to perform a receive and so, as a result, we're forced to wake *every stream associated with the queue* in order to avoid the entire queue becoming inactive if a stream fails to pick up the phone for some time. It's the equivalent of having extremely unreliable friends that all need reminding to feed your pet cat when you're on holiday, despite the fact that only one of them actually needs to do it, in order to minimise the chance of your cat starving to death.

I can't overstate how much of a issue this is for performance. It turns an operation that *should* be extremely fast (a rapid lock-free handover of a value to a receiver) into one that requires waking (and hence, repolling) potentially *hundreds* of async tasks. It significantly offsets much of the compelling performance potential async Rust has, especially given that channels are a pretty fundamental construct in the async world.

This isn't an isolated, implementation-specific problem with `flume` either. I've spoken to @stjepang and others. Their [`async-channel`](https://github.com/smol-rs/async-channel) crate has exactly the same problem (*all* `Stream`s associated with a channel must be woken whenever a message is inserted into the channel).

I don't pretend to have a solution to this issue by any means. Part of me thinks that there should be some method on `Stream` more akin to those on `Sink` that allow the stream to register interest in items and then de-register later on (the implementation of `Stream::next`, for example, could do this for each item future). However, it's not clear to me how and when those methods would be appropriately called to avoid this sort of problem (I assume at the start and end of stream drivers like `for_each` and `next`). At the very least, this would reduce this problem in scope significantly and make it no worse than the reduced version of the problem that already exists for futures that I mention above.

In fact, I suspect that this problem is more a fundamental limitation with the way `async` is currently done in Rust. There is no way (unlike with a thread) to correctly account for the fact that a future simply might not get polled again, and this results in either inefficient implementations or efficient but incorrect implementations that exhibit strange, non-local behaviours when dropping futures before they are ready. I suspect that, if one were to check, a *very significant* number of async-capable crates in the ecosystem will exhibit strange behaviour (or, worse, UB) if one were to drop or forget futures after their initial poll but before they become ready. I find it rather strange that there is not more documentation about this problem elsewhere (at least, I've not been able to find any), especially given [how](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nomicon/leaking.html) [much](http://cglab.ca/~abeinges/blah/everyone-poops/) documentation exists for similar problems. It's also worth to remember that this 'pre-pooping your pants' behaviour needs to be considered in async code *across every `.await` point* for *everything within scope* since each one could be a potential opportunity for the future to be dropped or, worse, leaked. However, this fact goes almost completely undocumented (not because it's an unknown issue, but probably because most people conceptualise `async` code as fundamentally the same as sync code with regard to flow control, despite this not being the case), no doubt resulting in a significant amount of unsafe code in the wild that appears safe at a glance but actually triggers UB (or invalidates some memory-safe but important invariant) if the future is not properly polled to completion due to some invariant that requires upholding across a `.await` point. It's worth noting that this isn't some unlikely quirk of writing unconventional code. Many future combinators rely on this behaviour to implement things like future racing. In my view, most users of async are woefully uninformed about this potential danger.

The prior paragraph's semi-rant aside, I think it would be amiss for this RFC to not at least mention the issue with regard to streams specifically given that streams exacerbate it to a significant extent (after scanning the RFC, I couldn't see a mention of anything similar to this problem).

comments.author: Diggsey
comments.body:
@zesterer I don't think this is a problem with streams specifically: a very similar problem occurs with async implementations of basic concurrency primitives like mutexes and read-write locks which do not rely on the `Stream` trait at all.

In general, it can happen any time you have an async construct that is accessed by more than two threads. In these situations the construct itself needs a way to drive progress without relying on one of the threads calling poll on some method (since that is not guaranteed to happen promptly).

In your MPMC channel example where you want to be able to stop receiving for a while, you would pass around a `Handle` to the channel, and only construct a `Receiver` from that handle when you want to start receving items. You would drop it when you want to stop. (And you can use the normal "close" + "keep receiving until end" pattern to ensure no items are lost when you want to stop receiving). This does not require changes to the `Stream` trait: you just shouldn't construct the type implementing `Stream` until ready to actually receive.

In more complex examples, it may be necessary to spawn the construct itself as a task, thereby decoupling it entirely from the threads which might access it.

comments.author: zesterer
Diggsey
comments.body:
> However, it becomes a particular acute problem with streams, because they, just as with iterators, may commonly be passed around with values only extracted on occasion. The same is not usually the case with futures because they are usually polled in-place.

I don't really agree with this. IME streams are either iterated directly or passed into another API just as commonly as futures are polled in-place.

> At the very least, optional methods on Stream with an empty default impl like thaw and freeze that might be called by users of the stream

Usually when a trait gets more than a couple of closely related methods it's a sign that they should be split up. Most uses of stream do not need to be paused and resumed so these methods should not be part of the Stream treait.

> Alternatively, an extension trait like trait PausableStream: Stream {} that is required for the implementation of non-contiguous polling of items such as that which occurs when using StreamExt::next intermittently would help to alleviate this problem, provided third-party crates like futures adhered to it.

This seems like a reasonable option. I don't see why the `futures` crate would need to do anything differently though. The crate defining `PausableStream` can provide appropriate implementations if any wrapper types from `futures` should themselves by pausable.

> As mentioned above, this is not something that can be solved from within the crate: it requires users to consciously avoid using the API in a manner that is suggestive of the API

To be clear, I was suggesting a change to the API of crates defining the MPMC channels such that the receiver side of the channel you get back does not directly implement `Stream`. It would simply have a method on to *get* a `Stream`. Then there's no need for a `PausableStream` or similar.

> EDIT: Instead of the current proposal, I'd suggest instead an API like this:

Again, I just don't think this belongs on `Stream`. In most cases a `Stream` only has a single consumer, so these methods are not relevant.

comments.author: skade
comments.body:
I think this points to an interesting problem in our communication as much as an API problem. I have, in the last years, frequently recommended people to not hide channels behind the stream interface, if at all possible. The choice of channel implementation is a) rather easy to change and b) "Everything is a stream" points to a space where it doesn't matter. Rather, I feel that choice of channel is a very important (and easily accessible) optimization technique, but none that happens in a generic fashion. To give an example of another domain: it is important that mysql and postgres are compatible, but you still want to choose them for features that the other doesn't have. So, on an application level, it's fine to call into one or the other.

Which is relevant for this RFC: is this interface recommended for (certain classes of) channels and if not, how will that be communicated?

comments.author: zesterer
comments.body:
> I don't really agree with this. IME streams are either iterated directly or passed into another API just as commonly as futures are polled in-place.

I think you're missing a crucial point here. This isn't about whether futures or streams are moved around more, it's about whether they're moved around more *after the first poll* (which registers the future/stream with the implementation's backend and is the cause of all of the aforementioned problems). The answer to this specific question is *very* different for streams and futures. I've never seen a future polled once in one location, then set aside for some time, and then polled again (apart from anything else, the `async`/`await` syntax doesn't even allow for it). Compare this to streams where this sort of operation is practically *the default* mode of use in many scenarios.

> Usually when a trait gets more than a couple of closely related methods it's a sign that they should be split up. Most uses of stream do not need to be paused and resumed so these methods should not be part of the Stream treait.

This is why I suggested a `PausableStream` trait. However, for it to be useful (i.e: it's needed to implement `StreamExt::next` and other non-consuming functions) it would need to be likewise defined in `std`.

> To be clear, I was suggesting a change to the API of crates defining the MPMC channels such that the receiver side of the channel you get back does not directly implement `Stream`. It would simply have a method on to _get_ a `Stream`. Then there's no need for a `PausableStream` or similar.

Yes, I understood your suggestion as such. I don't think that this is an acceptable compromise because it doesn't fundamentally change the fact that any such API will still reveal an implementer of `Stream`, and that there is no indication to the user that this type should not be used as if it were an iterator, despite it actually having different semantics due to the limitations of the `Stream` trait.

> Again, I just don't think this belongs on `Stream`. In most cases a `Stream` only has a single consumer, so these methods are not relevant.

And yet this is not the case: calling `stream.next().await` is not a pathological case, it is something that users might regularly want to do in the same sense that calling `iterator.next()` independently of a `for` loop or consuming function like `for_each` or `collect` is not a pathological case.

> I have, in the last years, frequently recommended people to not hide channels behind the stream interface, if at all possible.

Note that this limitation isn't just true for channels: it's also true for *anything* that pulls data out of some shared 'pool-like' resource. This definition covers a lot of the cases for which `Stream` is presumably intended to be useful, thereby nullifying or severely limiting its usefulness in those cases by imposing a design that requires an inefficient implementation.

comments.author: yoshuawuyts
zesterer
comments.body:
@yoshuawuyts 

> do you think this is an accurate summary

Broadly, yes. This design forces either an unnecessarily inefficient implementation (often wildly so) or an implementation is that simply incorrect.

> Would you be able to summarize your main point in a paragraph (ish), so we can consider it for inclusion in the RFC?

Futures are usually polled to completion in a single sitting. They may be passed around, but once the initial call to `Future::poll` occurs, the task will drive the future to completion 'ASAP' or drop the future early. Both are opportunities for the future to de-register itself from some internal data source. In contrast, streams are often polled until an item is extracted, and then left around (often with multiple `.await`s occurring between item extractions) to be polled for more items later. This means that the stream's implementation gets no chance to determine when it should start and stop listening to its internal data source, leading to inefficient implementations in a variety of circumstances.

> Specifically I'd like to understand whether adding the extensions you proposed would be possible as a future addition to Stream. I believe they would be?

On a technicality, yes: in the sense that compilation of a program would not fail if these additional methods were added later. *However*, they would be a breaking change in the sense that implementers of traits like `StreamExt` and anybody that wishes to poll a stream in a non-standard manner would need to remember to change their code to call them to avoid breaking the proper operation of the stream (not calling them means that the stream may not properly register/de-register itself). It is for this reason that I do not believe it to be a good idea to add them after `Stream` has already been stabilised.

> I think if you would take some time to turn this into a blog post it would be really good!

I'm currently rather busy this week but I'll try to find the time to do something like this soon, perhaps with examples of `Stream` implementations (and the problems associated with implementing them right now)!

comments.author: tmandry
comments.body:
> In contrast, streams are often polled until an item is extracted, and then left around (often with multiple `.await`s occurring between item extractions) to be polled for more items later.

@zesterer Thanks, this sentence was key for me understanding your concern. For understanding I also think it's worth highlighting that this problem only comes up in multi-consumer channels.

From reading this thread it seems like there are two things you would like to do, which are complementary but independent:

1. Limit the number of receivers to consider (wake) at any one time.
2. Move channel messages directly to space allocated for them by individual receivers.

The first could be accomplished with an API that registers/deregisters interest in a channel.

The second means that we are handing ownership to a receiver that (a) might be dropped without being polled, and (b) might not be polled for awhile.

(a) could be solved if we could somehow "hand back" the message to the channel on Drop and route to another receiver, though this could alter the ordering invariants of the channel (we couldn't guarantee that a single consumer always saw messages in the order they were sent). This would ensure that messages aren't lost, modulo receivers being leaked.

(b) can be solved with the same solution as (1) above; by only handing messages to receivers which are actively polling, we ensure each message is processed in a timely manner.

Does that sound right?

comments.author: Diggsey
comments.body:
> The answer to this specific question is very different for streams and futures. I've never seen a future polled once in one location, then set aside for some time, and then polled again (apart from anything else, the async/await syntax doesn't even allow for it).

This is exactly what the `select!` and `select_biased!` macros do (and they work in async contexts). Maybe this is slightly less common for futures than streams, but it's still reasonably common. Most of the non-trivial async code I write uses these macros.

> And yet this is not the case: calling stream.next().await is not a pathological case, it is something that users might regularly want to do in the same sense that calling iterator.next() independently of a for loop or consuming function like for_each or collect is not a pathological case.

I agree this is common. However, the problem you are describing only happens when streaming items from what you describe as a "shared pool", and in this situation futures have the same issue. ie. both futures and streams have issues when used in a "fan-out" manner.

Streams that are not used in a fan-out manner have no need to register and de-register interest in the underlying data source, and so it doesn't matter that calling `next().await` is a common pattern.

The most correct thing to do at these "fan-out" points is to spawn a task. Sure it has some overhead, but no more so than if Rust used an eager model for futures in the first place, and it can actually improve performance when there are a lot of consumers, because you don't have N threads competing for the same resource: you have one thread accessing the resource and distributing things to separate consumers.

comments.author: Nemo157
comments.body:
> In contrast, streams are often polled until an item is extracted, and then left around (often with multiple `.await`s occurring between item extractions) to be polled for more items later. This means that the stream's implementation gets no chance to determine when it should start and stop listening to its internal data source, leading to inefficient implementations in a variety of circumstances.

When the stream is left sitting around in this sort of situation, its last return value was `Poll::Ready(_)` and so it does not _have_ to be woken if a new item is available. Only once it is again polled and returns `Poll::Pending` does it need to once again be registered for notifications.

AFAICT a `Stream` implementation can already get the behaviour of `PausableStream` when just considering `Next`: do the `start_polling` operation when first polled while unregistered, do the `end_polling` operation when returning `Poll::Ready`.

The more complex cases are when you have polled, received `Pending`, then pause polling for a while; but this is analogous to running a `select!` where one branch is `stream.next()` and would require changes to `Future` to make _it_ pausable (and then propagate the pause through `Next` to the `Stream`).

comments.author: zesterer
comments.body:
To address the comments above:

> (a) could be solved if we could somehow "hand back" the message to the channel on Drop and route to another receiver, though this could alter the ordering invariants of the channel (we couldn't guarantee that a single consumer always saw messages in the order they were sent). This would ensure that messages aren't lost, modulo receivers being leaked.

The problem with this approach is that there's no reasonable guarantee that the stream will be dropped promptly after use (unlike a future, which is generally either polled to completion *or* (as @Diggsey mentioned when talking about `select!`) is partially polled and then quickly dropped).

> This is exactly what the `select!` and `select_biased!` macros do

It is not. These macros race futures against one-another and promptly drop both after one of them completes. Dropping is an opportunity for the future to deregister itself, but this opportunity is not forthcoming when using streams in a similar way.

> ie. both futures and streams have issues when used in a "fan-out" manner.

As I've consistently mentioned, this problem is not *unique* to streams, but it is *considerably worse* when using streams. You can technically still achieve this sort of weirdness with futures, yes: but it's not convenient to do so and it's definitely not easy to *accidentally* do so. This is in stark contrast to streams where seemingly innocent usage can end up producing extreme tail latencies in this manner.

> AFAICT a Stream implementation can already get the behaviour of PausableStream when just considering Next: do the start_polling operation when first polled while unregistered, do the end_polling operation when returning Poll::Ready.

The problem with this, as previously mentioned, is that registering and de-registering the stream for every item is likely to be very inefficient. Compare this to traits like `Iterator` that can override the implementation of methods like `for_each` to circumnavigate this issue. If streams can register themselves once at the beginning of a `for_each` and de-register at the end, the stream may be implemented considerably more efficiently.

I'd like to remind everyone here that my proposal has an extremely low implementation cost: for streams that do not use it, there is zero runtime cost (they are just empty methods, after all: the inliner will trivially remove them). In addition, the only people that need to care about them even existing are the developers of stream combinator/extension features like `StreamExt` and people that need to manually poll a stream (a relatively rare circumstance). These methods have no practical consequences for everyday users of streams, other than the fact that they provide the opportunity for more optimised stream implementations and reduce the risk of incorrect stream implementations.

comments.author: Diggsey
comments.body:
> It is not. These macros race futures against one-another and promptly drop both after one of them completes. Dropping is an opportunity for the future to deregister itself, but this opportunity is not forthcoming when using streams in a similar way.

`select!` and `select_biased!` don't drop the future, they just poll it:

https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=stable&mode=debug&edition=2018&gist=4ec5fec0348a81f49b1e38840e81d05d

comments.author: Nemo157
comments.body:
> If streams can register themselves once at the beginning of a `for_each` and de-register at the end, the stream may be implemented considerably more efficiently.

`for_each` takes a closure returning a future which may "block" for as long as it likes, so it would need to pause the stream before running that future and unpause it again afterwards. Similarly as soon as you have a `.then(_)` in a stream chain that `Then` stream would have to pre-emptively pause the prior stream in case the future it runs for each item "blocks" for too long. I don't think there are many situations in which you could poll a stream multiple times without pausing it between each poll.

comments.author: Thomasdezeeuw
KodrAus
comments.body: @rust-lang/libs I've dropped a link to [the FCP](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/2996#issuecomment-747664240) in the OP here, since it's gotten quite buried.

comments.author: zesterer
comments.body:
> Maybe Stream is simply not the correct abstraction for it. If you need to know (for performance reasons) what receivers are actively attempting to receive a value maybe a short-lived Future like the Stream::next method (now removed) returned would be a better fit.

Perhaps this is the case, but I think it would be a shame for `Stream` to not accommodate this common use-case when the cost of the features required to allow it to accommodate it is so low.

This also means that there is no longer a natural duality between `Stream` and `Iterator` as the RFC claims.

comments.author: nellshamrell
comments.body: Hello @rust-lang/libs :) For those of you who have not yet approved FCP in [this comment](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/2996#issuecomment-747664240) - are there any concerns you have or is there anything blocking approving this for FCP?

comments.author: nellshamrell
comments.body: @zesterer thank you for your comments, they encouraged us to think even more carefully about the design of this feature. I am going to leave the design as it is now as a minimal viable implementation, but we have your concerns about channels recorded here for future reference. I have no doubt we will be opening more RFCs around Async streams in the near future and we will certainly keep your concerns in mind. Thank you again, the community input and context is vital to any RFC.

comments.body: Gentle ping to @rust-lang/libs - this only needs one more approval for FCP!

title: build-std: always
body:
*[View all comments](https://triagebot.infra.rust-lang.org/gh-comments/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/3874)*

Add a new Cargo configuration option, `build-std = "always|never"`, which will unconditionally rebuild standard library dependencies. The set of standard library dependencies can optionally be customised with a new `build-std-crates` option. It also describes how Cargo (or external tools) should build the standard library crates on stable (i.e., which flags to pass and features to enable).

This proposal limits the ways the built standard library can be customised (such as by settings in the profile) and intends that the build standard library matches the prebuilt one (if available) as closely as possible.

**This RFC is is part of the [build-std project goal](https://rust-lang.github.io/rust-project-goals/2025h2/build-std.html) and a series of build-std RFCs:**

1. build-std context (rust-lang/rfcs#3873)
2. `build-std="always"` (this RFC)
3. Explicit standard library dependencies (rust-lang/rfcs#3875)
4. `build-std="compatible"` (RFC not opened yet)
5. `build-std="match-profile"` (RFC not opened yet)

There is also [a Zulip channel where you can ask questions about any of the build-std RFCs](https://rust-lang.zulipchat.com/#narrow/channel/516120-project-goals.2F2025h1.2Fbuild-std). This series of RFCs was drafted over many months with the help of stakeholders from many Rust project teams, we thank them for their help!

[FCP](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/3874#issuecomment-3715844925)

[Rendered](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/blob/master/text/3874-build-std-always.md)

so that is a bug still, but it can still be a security issue because

```rust
Privilege Escalation: If rustbot automatically assigns any user as a collaborator or assignees, it maybe grant them permissions they shouldn't have. In some configurations, assigning someone to a PR might trigger CI/CD pipelines that run with elevated secrets or write permissions, That is depending on the case.
```

title: RFC: Templating `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` to make it the parent of all target directories
body:
*[View all comments](https://triagebot.infra.rust-lang.org/gh-comments/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/3371)*

Introduce a new templating option for `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` to tell it to move the crate/workspace's target
directory into a crate/workspace-specific subdirectory of the configured path, allowing for easy exclusion from save tools for example.

[Rendered](https://github.com/poliorcetics/rfcs/blob/cargo-target-directories/text/3371-cargo-target-dir-templates.md) ([at d549cb12889d5a2c4a4dd64b5a8a0e3ee7b49aa0](https://github.com/poliorcetics/rfcs/blob/d549cb12889d5a2c4a4dd64b5a8a0e3ee7b49aa0/text/3371-cargo-target-dir-templates.md))
[FCP](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/3371#issuecomment-2258736204) ([previous FCP](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/3371#issuecomment-1602959262))

comments.author: ogoffart
comments.body:
What I would like to see is a "global" target directory for all projects, that way, common dependency artifacts would be shared. (Not all projects would need to compile syn and other common dependencies over and over again)

I think that would solve the problems that this RFC tries to solve. But I realise this has other challenges (different features set of dependencies, when/how to clean that cache, ...)

> `CARGO_TARGET_DIR_HOME`

Ah yes I like that idea, it mirrors `XDG_*_HOME`, thanks! I'll make the change when I get the time

comments.author: epage
comments.body:
Was just going to come here to propose a new name.  I was leaning towards `CARGO_TARGET_BASE_DIR`.  I chose putting "base" in there to indicate that this is the start/prefix of the target dir.  I considered "parent" but we might want to allow target directories to be under multiple levels to avoid there being too many items under a single directory.  I guess "root" might also work but that might be confused with just being the root of a single target directory.

`CARGO_TARGET_DIR_HOME` doesn't really convey the same thing, that we are holding multiple target directories under that location.  Yes, directories like `XDG_CACHE_HOME` convey that but we have existing semantics associated with the "TARGET_DIR" name.

comments.author: poliorcetics
comments.body: I think I addressed all currents comments, don't hesite to tell me if you find anything to clarify :)

comments.author: epage
comments.body:
With "cargo script", the target dir is roughly `$CARGO_HOME/target/<hash of .rs path>`. This RFC allows all packages to opt-in to something similar (but hashing workspace's `Cargo.toml` instead) by setting `$CARGO_TARGET_BASE_DIR`.  To completely unify the schemes, set `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=$CARGO_HOME/target`.

Current "cargo script"s hashing scheme is to use `Hash` and store it into an `u64` and then render that as hex.  We do `{hash[0..2]}/{hash[2..]}` for that fragment of the path (sharding by 256 should be enough for good filesystem performance so long as we have enough uniqueness on the first byte).  See rust-lang/cargo#12282.  There is no backlink or other hint for which package the target dir is used for.  The only thing you can do is run `cargo metadata` on your package and see the returned target dir.  [last-seen tracking](https://rust-lang.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/246057-t-cargo/topic/last-use.20tracking) might allow  us to track the backlink and we could consider a way to report it.

I would love to see the "cargo script" behavior made the default but I don't think that needs to happen as part of this RFC.

I'm sympathetic to the problem this is trying to solve, having previously dealt with having to set `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/target` for each of my builds due to security policies at a prior job, and the annoyances that caused.  Having `CARGO_TARGET_BASE_DIR` would have made this much nicer.

Overall, I feel like this is light enough that I don't think this would be a burden for the cargo team where it is currently at today.

My main caveats
- I do have a couple unresolved comments but they are not fundamental to the RFC
- I do not have strong thoughts on the exact resolution order / precedence between the different `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` sources and `CARGO_TARGET_BASE_DIR` sources (without a specific use case, I would lean towards "whatever is simplest to implement" but haven't looked at the code to see what that would be.
- We'll likely need to further hash out the hashing details, in tandem with "cargo script", on the path to stabilizing this feature.

@rfcbot fcp merge

comments.author: sunshowers
comments.body:
Apologies if these have already been discussed, finally catching up with this.

One thing I'm not totally clear about is how are we going to make the target directory discoverable? One of the ways targo provides a smooth interface is that you can continue to do `target/debug/<binary-name>` etc (though of course `cd target && cd ..` behaves differently). Losing this would be somewhat painful.

Also, if someone does a build using a version of cargo that supports `CARGO_TARGET_BASE_DIR`, then switches back to a version of cargo that doesn't support it, then we're going to be in a situation where there are two separate target directories. This breaks people's expectations that e.g. `target/debug/<binary-name>` refers to the most up-to-date contents. I can see this leading to a lot of confusion. (This is an issue targo doesn't have.)

comments.author: poliorcetics
comments.body:
Both of those are already a problem with `$CARGO_TARGET_DIR`, if it is set during one build and not the other. The first is less of a problem because `$CARGO_TARGET_DIR/debug/binary/...` is possible, but it depends on the value not changing and always being used so that the binary is up to date

Both of those are why I mentioned a (possible, to be bikeshedded) `cargo metadata --target-dir` that would return the exact target directory without an ending `\n`, allowing for things like `$(cargo metadata --target-dir)/debug/...`, which could easily be in a shell alias by the people needing it

comments.author: epage
comments.body:
> One thing I'm not totally clear about is how are we going to make the target directory discoverable? One of the ways targo provides a smooth interface is that you can continue to do `target/debug/<binary-name>` etc (though of course `cd target && cd ..` behaves differently). Losing this would be somewhat painful.

For me the questions are
- Is this a blocker for opt-in `CARGO_TARGET_BASE_DIR` (what the RFC currently covers)?
- Can we address this in the future before/if we make this the default?



> Both of those are already a problem with `$CARGO_TARGET_DIR`, if it is set during one build and not the other. The first is less of a problem because `$CARGO_TARGET_DIR/debug/binary/...` is possible, but it depends on the value not changing and always being used so that the binary is up to date
> 
> Both of those are why I mentioned a (possible, to be bikeshedded) `cargo metadata --target-dir` that would return the exact target directory without an ending `\n`, allowing for things like `$(cargo metadata --target-dir)/debug/...`, which could easily be in a shell alias by the people needing it

For anyone else wondering, this is under "Future Possibilities"

For me, a flag like this is a slippery slope.  We don't provide any other one-off queries at this time but instead opt-in to more information being included.

We have talked about supporting some kind of query language to report less information.  This would all still be subject to the output format.

comments.author: epage
comments.body:
> * How does this play with per-user caches discussed [here on Zulip](https://rust-lang.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/246057-t-cargo/topic/Per-user.20build.20caches). Or put it in this way, can per-user caches resolve part of the issues listed in motiviation?

With our [proposal for per-user caching](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/5931#issuecomment-1551478767), you'll still regularly use a target dir, so these would be orthogonal.

> One alternative approach to this: with some placeholder or env subsitution (e.g., https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/10789), we can have build.target-dir = "$HOME/.cache/<PROJECT_DIR>" so we perhaps don't need a new environment variable, and leave off the responsibility of resolving path conflict to users themselves.

We then need a variable for `<PROJECT_DIR>` that is unique.  We either have a hash of the manifest path, we do the REMAP proposal, or something else.  Otherwise, this falls back into being `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` and has to be setup per-project.

Virtual workspaces add wrinkles to this.

We also need to deal with path length issues

comments.author: sunshowers
comments.body:
> Both of those are already a problem with $CARGO_TARGET_DIR, if it is set during one build and not the other. The first is less of a problem because $CARGO_TARGET_DIR/debug/binary/... is possible, but it depends on the value not changing and always being used so that the binary is up to date

So coming at it from the point of a user: I don't use `$CARGO_TARGET_DIR` for exactly this reason, and I'm guessing most interactive users don't use that either. However I think it's safe to assume that many interactive users are going to want to use `CARGO_TARGET_BASE_DIR`, and I expect the symlink issue to be a blocker for many people. I know I wouldn't use `CARGO_TARGET_BASE_DIR` unless it automatically created a symlink.

Maybe the outcome here is that targo sticks around, using `CARGO_TARGET_BASE_DIR` if available, and creating a symlink. But in that world `CARGO_TARGET_BASE_DIR` wouldn't be a complete replacement for targo, just an aid that would make my life as the author of targo easier.

comments.author: poliorcetics
comments.body:
> So coming at it from the point of a user: I don't use $CARGO_TARGET_DIR for exactly this reason, and I'm guessing most interactive users don't use that either. However I think it's safe to assume that many interactive users are going to want to use CARGO_TARGET_BASE_DIR, and I expect the symlink issue to be a blocker for many people. I know I wouldn't use CARGO_TARGET_BASE_DIR unless it automatically created a symlink.

And I'm on the opposite side: I don't want any clutter in my repos, and `target`, even as a symlink, is clutter. I'm not saying your use case is wrong though, it's just very different to mine, so I'll need to think of something to merge the two

(By the way, I don't see how `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` is a problem in your case, just do `$CARGO_TARGET_DIR/...` once it has been set ? Or do you set it only for compilations ? I don't understand) 

As seen in https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/6100, there is also a need for an "out dir" that separates final binaries from the regular compilation artifacts.

I see a few possibilties, in no particular order:

1. Introduce an `create-target-dir-link` that would automatically create a symlink to the real target directory if it's not in the current workspace (that way `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target_renamed` would not have a symlink, but using `CARGO_TARGET_BASE_DIR` or `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/outside/workspace` would). People could then set it to `true` or `false`, either user-wide or on a per-workspace basis as they wish (and the default would have to decided, but that's not important for this discussion)

2. Delay any changes, pending a decision in the https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/6100 issue

3. Do nothing and encourage people to just use `cargo metadata --format-version 1 | jq .target_directory`. It certainly works and I personally already have aliases for it, but I can see why people wouldn't like it outside of scripts because of the extra dependency on `jq` and the general clunkiness.

4. Introduce a `cargo` command or subcommand that only yields the target dir, but @epage explained why that plan is not a good idea and I mostly agree with the arguments

comments.author: poliorcetics
comments.body:
> How does this play with per-user caches discussed [here on Zulip](https://rust-lang.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/246057-t-cargo/topic/Per-user.20build.20caches). Or put it in this way, can per-user caches resolve part of the issues listed in motiviation?

Probably part of them yes, but per-user cache don't provide isolation between projects no ?

> No symlink to old target dir. Agree on that it is already an issue with CARGO_TARGET_DIR. What about keeping uplifted final artifacts in the plain old <package-root>/target, and move other build caches otherwise? With that target becomes a canoncial path to find the final products, though we still need to find a way to "clean all final products" 😅.

Added forward links instead, allowing the existing workflow of `$ ./target/release/...` to still work

> One alternative approach to this: with some placeholder or env subsitution (e.g., https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/10789), we can have build.target-dir = "$HOME/.cache/<PROJECT_DIR>" so we perhaps don't need a new environment variable, and leave off the responsibility of resolving path conflict to users themselves.

How is `PROJECT_DIR` computed ? It's not provided by `cargo` right now and using the project name will easily conflict (I have three instances of my $work workspace and at least 2 of several open source projects I contribute to/forked)

If we end up with a viable way of providing this value, it would also be missing the back/forward links and associated metadata I talk about in the RFC, or we would have to modify `cargo`'s behaviour for the `target-dir` option.

If we provide all of the `PROJECT_DIR`, interpreting en vars and the target dir metadata we could probably drop this RFC yes but I don't see much activity on this.

Also, using `build.target-dir = "$HOME/.cache/<PROJECT_DIR>"` is incompatible with older cargo versions, which would make behaviour very strange during the transition period, though that is a temporary problem. `CARGO_TARGET_BASE_DIR`, as something that is only read by newer cargos, will not have this problem.

comments.author: poliorcetics

Currently, if the resolved `target dir` is invalid for cargo, it deletes it and recreates one, I would expect the same for the `./target` link to wherever: cargo would check after creating its file lock and fix it if it's an invalid symlink. If it's a directory, leave it alone maybe ?

comments.author: ensc
comments.body:
Generating the `target` symlink sounds wrong to me:

- `target` was not created before when `build.target-dir` was set; now, it is always created
- it pollutes sources; related question:
  - what happens, when cargo can not write to source directory (read-only mount, permissions) and can not create the link there?
- unclear semantics when a `target` filesystem object already exists

and unnecessary:

- when people do not use `CARGO_TARGET_BASE_DIR`, they will have their old `target` directory and their tools will still work
- when they use it, they know about the new semantics and have to adopt their tools
- when something in the buildsystem assumes `target`, then it is broken and fails already now when `build.target-dir` is set

comments.author: poliorcetics
comments.body:
> target was not created before when build.target-dir was set; now, it is always created

No. It's explicitely mentioned as not being the case in the RFC. It only applies for the new `CARGO_TARGET_BASE_DIR`, not for `CARGO_TARGET_DIR`, see section **Providing forward links**

> it pollutes sources

There is an option to deactivate it, mentioned in the same section as above

> unclear semantics when a target filesystem object already exists

Same as current cargo. if a `target` file exists already: it fails, that's not unclear semantics.

```
cd /tmp/; cargo new test-target; cd test-target; echo "abcd" > target; cargo build
error: failed to create directory `/private/tmp/test-target/target/debug`

Caused by:
  Not a directory (os error 20)
```

> when they use it, they know about the new semantics and have to adopt their tools

The whole goal and previous remarks where that it was wanted, just like `targo` does it. The symlink is intended for *humans* so they can type `./target/debug/my-bin` without having to find the hash or whatever scheme is used for the actual target dir.

> when something in the buildsystem assumes target, then it is broken and fails already now when build.target-dir is set

Yes, that's also mentioned in the RFC, also in the previously mentioned section ...

comments.author: epage
comments.body:
@poliorcetics we discussed this in today's cargo team meeting

We are generally in favor as this is a principled 80%/20% solution.  While there are likely better, more complete solutions for each motivation, this is orthogonal  to those and gets us there much sooner.
- Relative CARGO_TARGET_BASE_DIR, especially `target-base-dir` relative to the config file, was discussed but we agreed that this RFC doesn't have to be blocked on it
- Substitutions came up to allow customizing what gets used for the path (which was also discussed earlier in this RFC)
  - While there can be concerns over compatibility, they are likely minor and can likely be handed like we are doing for build scripts (https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/pull/12201 adds new syntax but a prior PR made the new syntax an error)
  - If we model substitutions based on [index configuration's `dl` field](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/registry-index.html#index-configuration), we might be able to support this in `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` directly
  - A benefit to overloading `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` is it reduces the need for fallback schemes
  - A downside to overloading `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` is if any other programs read it, they'll do the wrong thing.

Based on the above, I'd recommend adding the following solutions and propose whether it should be the agreed-on solution or left to Alternatives
- `CARGO_TARGET_BASE_DIR` will auto-append a path if none of the following keys are present...
  - This could also just be a future-possibility
- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` will substitute the following keys...
  - To reduce the chance of bad behavior, we'll have a transition period where cargo will error if those keys are present.  After that, they will be substituted
  - This does run into problems with the forward link proposal.  Maybe we add the config for target-dir itself and have it off-by-default?

comments.author: epage
comments.body: FYI my direct edits to the RFC were intended to capture some of the existing discussion that hadn't been captured yet and were not intended to change the proposal itself.

comments.author: poliorcetics
comments.body:
> Based on the above, I'd recommend adding the following solutions and propose whether it should be the agreed-on solution or left to Alternatives
> 
>     * `CARGO_TARGET_BASE_DIR` will auto-append a path if none of the following keys are present...
>       
>       * This could also just be a future-possibility
> 
>     * `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` will substitute the following keys...
>       
>       * To reduce the chance of bad behavior, we'll have a transition period where cargo will error if those keys are present.  After that, they will be substituted
>       * This does run into problems with the forward link proposal.  Maybe we add the config for target-dir itself and have it off-by-default?

I'm not sure I understand the ideas proposed here. Especially, I'm not sure what keys would be good to have or not. Additionally, do we want a generic key-substitution language here or not ?

Also, a substitution system seems much bigger than this RFC, since adding it would probably raise demands about what config to add it to (or not). I don't feel it's necessary for a first approach, the env var form can be used if substitution is wanted, just like for current `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` no ?

> FYI my direct edits to the RFC were intended to capture some of the existing discussion that hadn't been captured yet and were not intended to change the proposal itself.

No worries, thanks for working on this!

comments.body:
Missed responding about the overloading of `CARGO_TARGET_DIR`, sorry.



> * A benefit to overloading `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` is it reduces the need for fallback schemes
> 
> * A downside to overloading `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` is if any other programs read it, they'll do the wrong thing.

Ideally all third parties would use `cargo metadata` but I don't think putting my head in the sand will fix reality that way so a new config variable seems "safer" at first.

The issue of course, is "doing the wrong thing" could fail in very strange way if the two configs are not in sync and a tool uses the old one without taking into account the new one.

I have no idea what the desired behaviour here, I'm open to suggestions and opinions.

comments.author: epage
comments.body:
> Also, a substitution system seems much bigger than this RFC, since adding it would probably raise demands about what config to add it to (or not). I don't feel it's necessary for a first approach, the env var form can be used if substitution is wanted, just like for current `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` no ?

Technically, `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` with support for substituting a `{manifest-path-hash}` (name to be bikeshed-ed) would the the smallest form of this RFC, as the config field already exists (not needing new flags or precedence schemas) and it'd be building on our [prior art](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/registry-index.html#index-configuration) for the substitutions.

As I said for `CARGO_TARGET_BASE_DIR` as the solution, substitutions can likely be a future possibility.

> I'm not sure I understand the ideas proposed here. Especially, I'm not sure what keys would be good to have or not. Additionally, do we want a generic key-substitution language here or not ?

For using `CARGO_TARGET_BASE_DIR` or `CARGO_TARGET_DIR`, the minimum keys would be the one for hashing the manifest path.  We could consider also supporting `HOME`, `CARGO_HOME`, etc.

As pointed out, we have a substitution system already designed.  We'd just defer to that.

comments.author: kornelski
comments.body:
Cargo has these dilemmas about the location of the target dir, because Cargo has conflated two concepts:

1. intermediate build products (3rd party dependencies, object files, compiler-internal caches)
2. final build artifacts (linked build targets of the current workspace only)
 
Users want 1 in a centralized location to deduplicate/reuse intermediate products (files they never touch). And users want 2 in ./target to run or copy the files easily, from a convenient location that has no global naming clashes. 

As long as Cargo will continue to dump both kinds of products into the same directory, it will get complaints about making build results difficult to reach, or even overwriting each other, while at the same time getting requests to unify the directory. 

Adding a hash to the path is not solving either of these problems. The hash keeps common dependencies built redundantly and puts the final artifacts in an inconvenient location.

> 
> Users want 1 in a centralized location to deduplicate/reuse intermediate products (files they never touch). And users want 2 in ./target to run or copy the files easily, from a convenient location that has no global naming clashes.
> 
> As long as Cargo will continue to dump both kinds of products into the same directory, it will get complaints about making build results difficult to reach, or even overwriting each other, while at the same time getting requests to unify the directory.
> 
> Adding a hash to the path is not solving either of these problems. The hash keeps common dependencies built redundantly and puts the final artifacts in an inconvenient location.

This isn't a full solution to this problem, but (having used targo full time for over a year) it solves several other problems that are roughly as important.

> 
>

> 
> As long as Cargo will continue to dump both kinds of products into the same directory, it will get complaints about making build results difficult to reach, or even overwriting each other, while at the same time getting requests to unify the directory.

I think you misunderstood the RFC.

Point `2. final build artifacts (linked build targets of the current workspace only)` is already being worked by the out-dir/artifact-dir discussion, which is mentioned in the RFC, and out of scope for this one.

> Users want 1 in a centralized location to deduplicate/reuse intermediate products (files they never touch).

That is already possible, always has been I think, just by setting a `cargo-target-dir` globally. The RFC discusses this and also why this can be an issue for some (e.g all the people that commented on the original issue, which is linked at the start of the RFC).

> Adding a hash to the path is not solving either of these problems. The hash keeps common dependencies built redundantly and puts the final artifacts in an inconvenient location.

Because the problems you're describing are **not** what this RFC is proposing to solve.

What this RFC is proposing is to gather all target directories under a common "base" path while keeping them separate for each project, for the purposes of caching, cleaning local repositories, sharing build artifacts via NFS or any other usage that people will think of or have mentioned in the original issue.

This RFC proposes an *opt-in* option: it won't make life harder for people that don't use it, just like lots of people don't override their `cargo-target-dir` path and so don't have to deal with it.

Separating intermediate and final build artifacts or making them (either or) easy to access are not goals: those are either cargo issues or RFCs already.

comments.author: epage
comments.body:

To add to this, I want to eventually change this *but* I feel its dependent on finalizing `--out-dir` / `--artifact-dir` which I suspect will eventually lead to separate locations for intermediate artifacts and final artifacts.  The big question to deal with is "what is a final artifact?".   For most people, its just bins and dylibs.  But some also consider examples, tests, etc.  However, thats a topic for `--artifact-dir`.

title: More methods for std::time::Duration
body:
Right now, we have:

* `from_secs`
* `from_millis`
* `as_secs`
* `subsec_nanos`

It'd be nice if we also had:

* `from_hours` (note: potential for overflow)
* `from_mins` (note: potential for overflow)
* `from_micros`
* `from_nanos`
* `as_hours`
* `as_mins`
* `subsec_millis`
* `subsec_micros`

And potentially also:

* `from_days` (note: potential for overflow)
* `as_days`
* `as_millis` (note: potential for overflow)
* `as_micros` (note: potential for overflow)
* `as_nanos` (note: potential for overflow)

(Note that `as_millis` is already suggested by #1545).

`time::Duration` already has a lot of these, and it'd be nice if the libstd version did too. These don't seem that hard to implement or maintain, and they seem useful enough to have.

comments.author: polyfloyd
nagisa
comments.body:
The [duration reform rfc](
https://github.com/nox/rust-rfcs/blob/master/text/1040-duration-reform.md)
explicitly avoids adding units such as hours or days.

On Mar 17, 2017 4:33 PM, "polyfloyd" <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> Yes, time::Duration is lacking quite some functionality.
>
> Go <https://golang.org/pkg/time/#Duration> uses constants to work with
> durations. Maybe we could introduce this idea in Rust:
>
> let four_seconds = time::SECOND * 4;
> let five_hundred_millis = time::MILLISECOND * 500;
> let one_and_a_half_second = time::SECOND + time::SECOND / 2;
> let one_day = time::HOUR * 24;
>
> Similarly, as_* could be realized by division and subsec_* by the
> remainder.
>
> —
> You are receiving this because you are subscribed to this thread.
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub
> <https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/issues/1917#issuecomment-287370092>,
> or mute the thread
> <https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/AApc0kQw6293PzpBZgrCO2PtdzOoT3Ffks5rmpmmgaJpZM4MIAni>
> .
>

comments.author: mqudsi
comments.body:
The reason why these larger, more-confusing units *should* be included (IMHO) is precisely because they are hard to get right. I think C#'s `TimeSpan` is a good model for `Duration`; it provides an easy way to go from logical units to system time and abstracts away all the things that could go wrong. Conversation to and from "higher order" units via the `from_xxx` and `as_xxx` methods is a great boon to developers of "real" software, and if implemented properly, could save a whole class of problems for developers down the road.

If this should not be included in core, there should at least be a blessed crate for this; already there is a huge and unwieldy gap between std and the `time` crate, which is a source of enormous confusion to the entire community.

comments.author: AaronKutch
comments.body: I want to add a set of `uX::wrapping_div_rem(lhs, rhs) -> (uX, uX)` functions as a way to guarantee that `__udivmodti4` and the like are called, and not have to rely on the compiler backend to combine operations. However, someone encountered an [LLVM error](https://github.com/rust-lang/compiler-builtins/issues/390) where the target `sparc-unknown-linux-gnu` cannot lower any function that returns a tuple of two `u128`s. If the function were added, wouldn't that completely break 32-bit SPARC targets unless the LLVM issue is resolved?

comments.author: programmerjake
comments.body: It seems to work fine from clang: https://gcc.godbolt.org/z/8xbrWE

comments.author: parraman
comments.body:
I don't know if it's useful, but I have compiled the following code and generated the corresponding LLVM IR:

```rust
#[no_mangle]
fn test_128(one: u128, two: u128) -> (u128, u128) {
    let division = one / two;
    (division + 40000000, division - 100000)
}
```

The LLVM IR is:
```llvm
; Function Attrs: minsize nounwind optsize
define { i128, i128 } @test_128(i128 %one, i128 %two) unnamed_addr #0 {
start:
  %_6 = icmp eq i128 %two, 0
  br i1 %_6, label %panic, label %bb1, !prof !1, !misexpect !2

bb1:                                              ; preds = %start
  %division = udiv i128 %one, %two
  %_7 = add i128 %division, 40000000
  %_9 = add i128 %division, -100000
  %0 = insertvalue { i128, i128 } undef, i128 %_7, 0
  %1 = insertvalue { i128, i128 } %0, i128 %_9, 1
  ret { i128, i128 } %1
```
NOTE: I have removed the panic block since I believe it shouldn't be what is causing the error.

The error output generated by rustc is:
```
LLVM ERROR: unable to allocate function return #6
```
I have generated the LLVM IR with the flag `--emit=llvm-ir`. The equivalent function in LLVM IR generated from clang is [here](https://gcc.godbolt.org/z/n75a14).

Thanks a lot!

comments.author: NCGThompson
comments.body:
I would like to point out that div_rem methods may have value even without intrinsic functions to implement them.
* Most importantly, the methods might just help with convenience or readability.
* The standard library is always compiled with opt-level=3, so the optimization will exist even in debug mode.
* There is a little bit of room for optimization outside of the intrinsics. Ex: [wrapping_div_rem_euclid()](https://godbolt.org/#g:!((g:!((g:!((h:codeEditor,i:(filename:'1',fontScale:14,fontUsePx:'0',j:1,lang:rust,selection:(endColumn:2,endLineNumber:16,positionColumn:2,positionLineNumber:16,selectionStartColumn:2,selectionStartLineNumber:16,startColumn:2,startLineNumber:16),source:'%23%5Binline(never)%5D%0Apub+fn+wrapping_div_rem(dend:+i64,+isor:+i64)+-%3E+(i64,+i64)+%7B%0A++++(%0A++++++++dend.wrapping_div_euclid(isor),%0A++++++++dend.wrapping_rem_euclid(isor)%0A++++)%0A%7D%0A%0A%23%5Binline(never)%5D%0Apub+fn+another_wrapping_div_rem(dend:+i64,+isor:+i64)+-%3E+(i64,+i64)+%7B%0A++++let+abs_isor+%3D+isor.wrapping_abs()%3B+//+guarantees+no+overflow%0A++++let+mut+q+%3D+dend+/+abs_isor%3B+//+divide+by+zero+check%0A++++let+r+%3D+dend+%25+abs_isor%3B+//+all+checks+optimized+out%0A++++q+%3D+if+isor+%3C+0+%7B+q.wrapping_abs()+%7D+else+%7B+q+%7D%3B%0A++++(q,+r)%0A%7D'),l:'5',n:'0',o:'Rust+source+%231',t:'0')),k:50,l:'4',n:'0',o:'',s:0,t:'0'),(g:!((h:compiler,i:(compiler:r1740,filters:(b:'0',binary:'1',binaryObject:'1',commentOnly:'0',debugCalls:'1',demangle:'0',directives:'0',execute:'1',intel:'0',libraryCode:'0',trim:'1'),flagsViewOpen:'1',fontScale:14,fontUsePx:'0',j:1,lang:rust,libs:!(),options:'-C+opt-level%3D3',overrides:!(),selection:(endColumn:1,endLineNumber:1,positionColumn:1,positionLineNumber:1,selectionStartColumn:1,selectionStartLineNumber:1,startColumn:1,startLineNumber:1),source:1),l:'5',n:'0',o:'+rustc+1.74.0+(Editor+%231)',t:'0')),k:50,l:'4',n:'0',o:'',s:0,t:'0')),l:'2',n:'0',o:'',t:'0')),version:4)

Since the compiler doesn't recognize the hard coded div function, ethnum-rs has a set of [div_rem methods](https://github.com/nlordell/ethnum-rs/blob/ad7db74f3a0b13bd3f036423961fe692c9eb1e21/src/int.rs#L276-L627) analogous to the integer methods in the standard library. I suggest taking a look at the API and documentation as an example of what the div_rem methods could look like. The implementing code itself isn't as applicable because there were different intrinsics to work with.
