comments.author: joshtriplett
comments.body: We talked about this at length in today's @rust-lang/lang meeting, and had several thoughts. We were all generally in favor of arbitrary self types, but also had a number of questions and potential improvements. Several of us are going to post our thoughts, in separate comments.

comments.author: scottmcm
comments.body:
In general this is looking great, and I'd definitely like this to become something possible.

I just have one thing about pointers to discuss.

---

I'm having trouble understanding how both of these make sense at the same time:

> [`Receiver`] is also implemented for &T, &mut T, *const T and *mut T.

> `Weak<T>` and `NonNull<T>` were not supported by the prior unstable arbitrary self types support, but they share the property that it may be desirable to implement method calls to T using them as self types. Unfortunately they also share the property that these types have many Rust methods using `self`, `&self` or `&mut self`.

We also have, if I counted correctly, 42 methods on `*const _` right now.  If we go [back in time](https://doc.rust-lang.org/1.10.0/std/primitive.pointer.html) there were far fewer, but today it seems like there's enough that we can't assume it's a full set -- the strict provenance stuff added a whole bunch relatively recently, for example.

---

More generally, what about all the other possible types in the standard library?  I could imagine someone reasonably wanting to have `self: MaybeUninit<Self>`, for example.  But since that doesn't `Deref`, it also has loads of methods on it, and thus it probably shouldn't be `Receiver` either?  Same for `Mutex` or `Vec` or ...

Thus my instinct is that we should require that they make something like the `CppRef` type you describe in the Motivation, rather than having a pointer be a receiver directly.  That doesn't seem *that* unreasonable, either, since most of the methods on pointers aren't things that one should be doing on a C++ reference anyway.

Then we stick with the "you shouldn't do this if you have methods" rule, which means no `impl<T> Receiver for Vec<T>`, but it *could* mean `impl<T> Receiver for ManuallyDrop<T>`, or similar.  (Well, I guess that one has `Deref` so it'd be caught by the proposed blanket impl.)

Just as a SHOULD, not as a MUST, in the language *rule* though.  If you really want inherent methods -- that always win in resolution -- on something `impl Receiver`, I think that's fine, just not for standard library things.  An external crate could always do a major version bump to add more inherent methods like that if it really wanted to.

---

Unless there's some strong reason that methods need pointer receivers *directly* that I'm missing?  My instinct is that if you can implement `Receiver` for your own type, that's fine, and it's not needed on raw pointers directly.

comments.author: joshtriplett
comments.body:
The restriction on `NonNull` and `Weak` seems unfortunate, for multiple reasons.

- These are types that make a lot of sense to use as receivers.
- It seems like raw pointers *also* have lots of methods, and we may well add more in the future, so it's not obvious why it's OK to have raw-pointer receivers but not `NonNull`/`Weak` receivers. Is there a reason why the same considerations don't apply?

I'd be enthusiastically in favor of this, but I'd like to find a way that we can allow it for `NonNull` and `Weak` as well, and in general for receivers that do have methods people might want to call.

When you add a new method to a trait, that's not a breaking change, because inherent methods get priority.

However, with `Deref`, we already have the rule that methods directly on the type have priority over methods on the `Deref::Target`. Which makes it a breaking change to add methods directly on the type.

For Receiver, the other way around seems more robust against breakage, since it seems acceptable for a method `impl SomeType { fn method(self: FooPtr<Self>); }` to shadow `FooPtr::method`. However, that would be incompatible with existing usage with `Deref`, and I don't see any way we could get there even if we wanted to.

That said, I think we should consider (in order from most to least preferable):

- Adding *some* way to make it not a breaking change to add methods on a type that implements `Receiver`, or to make it reasonable to put it in the category of "acceptable breakage" like we do inference failure. Then, implement `Receiver` for `NonNull` and `Weak`.
- Implement `Receiver` for `NonNull` and `Weak` anyway, if we're going to have it for raw pointers. In particular, I don't think it makes sense to have it for raw pointers and *not* for `NonNull`.
- Making raw pointers not a `Receiver`. Same argument: it doesn't make sense to have it for raw pointer and not `NonNull`.
- Add a way for us to transition over an edition to moving the various methods on raw pointers and `NonNull` to associated functions. That seems like a massive amount of churn, though. I'm only mentioning this possibility for completeness, in case it inspires some better idea.

comments.author: Nadrieril
comments.body:
> it seems acceptable for a method `impl SomeType { fn method(self: FooPtr<Self>); }` to shadow `FooPtr::method`. However, that would be incompatible with existing usage with `Deref`

Why is it incompatible? From what I understand we could treat `Deref` and `Receiver` differently when deciding shadowing precedence. The only types where this could break are types that are already `Receiver` today, i.e. `Box`, `Rc` and friends, but they already don't have inherent methods so that's fine.

comments.author: madsmtm
comments.body:
> Implement `Receiver` for `NonNull` and `Weak`

Once we do those, one might ask: why don't we also allow `self: Cell<Self>`, `self: [Self]`, `self: HashSet<Self>` and so on?

So yeah, definitely a good point, I think the motivation for allowing raw pointers was also because it lined up nicely with the proposed changes to the method resolution, but we're [changing those anyway](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/3519#discussion_r1400808795), so it might not make so much sense any more?

Will have to consider this further!

comments.author: adetaylor
comments.body:
Thanks for all the discussion around `NotNull`, `Weak` etc. I thought I'd take the liberty of summarizing the different perspectives to see if it helps us reach a consensus:

* The existing unstable `arbitrary_self_types` feature allows use of raw pointers as self types (but not `NotNull`, `Weak` etc. because they don't implement `Deref`)
* @scottmcm feels [we shouldn't have raw pointer self types, but require people to wrap those pointers in a newtype wrapper](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/3519#issuecomment-1823266138) (this is my instinct too but just because I'm automatically nervous of larger changes!)
* @nikomatsakis is of the view that [raw pointer self types are important for better quality unsafe code](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/3519#issuecomment-1813713761) and I've heard related points from @Manishearth who says:
  >  raw pointer receivers are quite important for the future of safe Rust, because stacked borrows makes it illegal to materialize references in many positions, and there are a lot of operations (like going from a raw pointer to a raw pointer to a field) where you don't need to or want to do that.
* @joshtriplett says we should implement `Receiver` for `NonNull` and `Weak` if we implement it for raw pointers, but hopes we can find a way to avoid it being a compatibility break

I think: it seems like there's enough desire for raw pointers, `NotNull`, `Weak` etc. as `self` types that we should try pretty hard to find a way to avoid compatibility breaks, even if it means we do something a bit unexpected from the user's perspective. There are some proposals around that [in this comment thread about the RFC](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/3519#discussion_r1383106315) which I haven't had a chance to properly get my head around, let alone work into RFC revisions! But further comments on that thread are very much appreciated.

(Edit: that linked comment thread now contains an actual proposal and a trial implementation, please take a look)

comments.author: joshtriplett
comments.body:
> > it seems acceptable for a method `impl SomeType { fn method(self: FooPtr<Self>); }` to shadow `FooPtr::method`. However, that would be incompatible with existing usage with `Deref`
> 
> Why is it incompatible? From what I understand we could treat `Deref` and `Receiver` differently when deciding shadowing precedence.

I mean that it'd be confusing if some type `SmartPtr` that implements `Deref` (and thus gets the blanket impl of `Receiver`) has methods that take precedence over methods of the type it `Deref`s to if they take a `T`, but *don't* take precedence if they take a `SmartPtr<T>`.

comments.author: Nadrieril
comments.body:
> I mean that it'd be confusing if some type SmartPtr that implements Deref (and thus gets the blanket impl of Receiver) has methods that take precedence over methods of the type it Derefs to if they take a T, but don't take precedence if they take a SmartPtr<T>.

I think of it as "most specific impl first" (with caveat and details [here](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/3519#discussion_r1403770025)). It makes sense in my head, it's kind of like specialization: a `foo(Box<Self>)` takes priority over a generic `foo(self)` on `Box<T>`. I think we can teach this in a way that's not too confusing.

comments.author: traviscross
comments.body:
@rustbot labels -I-lang-nominated

This was discussed in the T-lang meeting on 2023-11-27.  Some members have left comments above deriving from this.

The general feeling seemed to be that we wanted to find some way to enable this, including for raw pointers, `NonNull`, etc., but we were feeling unsure about the path to get there.

In light of this, probably further reflection and discussion on this thread or in Zulip is warranted, and a revision to the RFC that addresses this and proposed a plan here would be in order.

Once that is done, please renominate for T-lang so that the team can discuss the changes.

comments.author: adetaylor
comments.body:
@traviscross Thank you! Yes.

Another periodic status update:

* I've figured out (I think) how to solve [the problem with non-dereffable types](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/3519#discussion_r1407548885) - I intend to write it up. I've even made some (non-fancy) diagrams of how it all works right now and how it will change.
* I think we can make it work for `NonNull` and friends using [the proposals in this thread](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/3519#discussion_r1383106315)... but it's not quite working yet, TBD
* Together, the two things above will require a significant rewrite of the Reference section of this RFC (maybe even with diagrams, I don't know if that's normal in Rust RFCs)
* In parallel, the lifetime elision anomalies are being [explored here](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/117967) including a crater run

comments.author: adetaylor
adetaylor
comments.body:
Here's how I think method resolution should work after this RFC. This approach allows us to use `NonNull` method receivers,  using something _approximating_ the [approach in this thread](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/3519#discussion_r1383106315), and solves the problem highlighted around [non-dereffable types here](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/3519#discussion_r1407548885). It also solves the problem in the last comment.

First, this assumes that there is a blanket impl of `Receiver` for `T: Deref`. To abuse UML a little bit, things look like this:

```mermaid
classDiagram
  Receiver <|-- Deref
  Receiver <|-- CppRef
  note for CppRef "or other types that\nimpl Receiver but not Deref\ne.g.NonNull"
  Deref <|-- Box
  note for Box "or other types that\nimpl Deref\ne.g.Rc"
  class usize
  note for usize "or other types that\nimpl neither"
```

So, for any given type, say `A<B<C<D<E>>>>`, there's a chain of `Deref`s which can be followed, and a chain of `Receiver`s. The `Receiver` chain is always just an extension of the `Deref` chain.

```mermaid
graph LR
  A --Receiver--> B
  A --Deref--> B
  B --Receiver--> C
  B --Deref--> C
  C --Receiver--> D
  D --Receiver-->E
```

### How method probing works now

This is roughly a diagrammatic version of [this excellent page](https://rustc-dev-guide.rust-lang.org/method-lookup.html#method-lookup) though it includes a few different details which will be important for this RFC. *This is all, of course, quite simplified*.

Everything is driven from [`autoderef`](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/4d1bd0db7f489b22c6d8aa2385937a95412c015b/compiler/rustc_hir_analysis/src/autoderef.rs#L27) which knows how to iterate through the chain of `Deref` links shown above. This is used to check valid receiver types (in [`wfcheck.rs`](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/4d1bd0db7f489b22c6d8aa2385937a95412c015b/compiler/rustc_hir_analysis/src/check/wfcheck.rs#L4)) and twice in method probing, in [`probe.rs`](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/4d1bd0db7f489b22c6d8aa2385937a95412c015b/compiler/rustc_hir_typeck/src/method/probe.rs#L4). At present, the same chain of `Deref`s is used in all three places.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  subgraph autoderef
     direction TB
     A --Deref--> B
     B --Deref--> C
  end
  subgraph wfcheck[wfcheck.rs]
     wfchecker[Confirm valid self types in each method]
  end
  autoderef-->wfchecker
  subgraph probe[probe.rs]
    method_autoderef_steps
    candidate_steps[(list of candidate steps with\nself_ty\nnumber of derefs)]
    method_autoderef_steps-->candidate_steps
    probe_op[probe_op & various\n'functions calling push_candidate]
    candidate_steps-->probe_op
    candidates[(list of candidate methods with\nassociated item &\nxformed self ty)]
    probe_op-->candidates
    candidates-->pick_all_method
    pick_all_method[pick which candidate\nby iterating types and\nlooking for matching methods.\nAt each step]
    candidate_steps--work out types to which\nreceiver can be converted-->pick_all_method
  end
  autoderef-->method_autoderef_steps
  ambiguity[Report method ambiguity error]
  confirm[confirm.rs]
  pick_all_method--exactly 1 method-->confirm
  pick_all_method--multiple methods-->ambiguity
  e0282[error 0282: type annotation needed]
  method_autoderef_steps--final type was a type variable-->e0282
```

Note in particular that the `candidate_steps` are effectively used twice.

### How this changes with arbitrary self types

With this RFC, the following changes occur:
* Sometimes we follow the chain of `Receiver` instead of `Deref`. Specifically,
   * We do this in `wfcheck.rs` when checking for well-formed `self` types
   * We also do this in `probe.rs` when assembling the list of candidate functions, _but not_ when we are working out the types to which the actual method call receiver can be converted. For that we continue to use `Deref`.
* Sometimes, if there are multiple candidates, we pick one but show a warning about the ambiguity. (This is what allows us to use `impl Receiver for NonNull` despite the fact that we might want to add methods to `NonNull`in future).

So the picture changes like this:


```mermaid
flowchart TB
  subgraph autoderef[autoderef using Deref]
     direction TB
     A --Deref--> B
     B --Deref--> C
  end
  subgraph autoderef2[autoderef using Receiver]
     direction TB
     A2[A] --Receiver--> B2[C]
     B2[B] --Receiver--> C2[C]
     C2[C] --Receiver--> D2[D]
     D2[D] --Receiver--> E2[E]
  end
  subgraph wfcheck[wfcheck.rs]
     wfchecker[Confirm valid self types in each method]
  end
  autoderef2-->wfchecker
  subgraph probe[probe.rs]
    method_autoderef_steps[method_autoderef_steps using Deref]
    method_autoderef_steps2[method_autoderef_steps using Receiver]
    candidate_steps[(list of candidate steps with\nself_ty\nnumber of derefs)]
    candidate_steps2[(list of candidate steps with\nself_ty\nnumber of receiver hops)]
    method_autoderef_steps-->candidate_steps
    method_autoderef_steps2-->candidate_steps2
    probe_op[probe_op & various\n'functions calling push_candidate]
    candidate_steps2-->probe_op
    candidates[(list of candidate methods with\nassociated item &\nxformed self ty)]
    probe_op-->candidates
    candidates-->pick_all_method
    pick_all_method[pick which candidate\nby iterating types and\nlooking for matching methods.\nAt each step]
    candidate_steps--work out types to which\nreceiver can be converted-->pick_all_method
  end
  autoderef-->method_autoderef_steps
  autoderef2-->method_autoderef_steps2
  ambiguity[Report method ambiguity error]
  confirm[confirm.rs]
  confirm_warn[confirm but warn of ambiguity]
  pick_all_method--exactly 1 method-->confirm
  pick_all_method--exactly 1 'innermost' method-->confirm_warn
  pick_all_method--multiple methods-->ambiguity
  e0282[error 0282: type annotation needed]
  method_autoderef_steps--final type was a type variable-->e0282
```

'Innermost' in this context means a viable candidate method on the 'innermost' type, that is, the type we got via the largest number of steps along the `Receiver` chain.

In practice, the code isn't this complex - we don't have to store two different lists of `candidate_steps` - but logically this is what we're doing.

### More detail on how we support the `NotNull<T>` case

`pick_all_method` iterates through the chain of types built by the `Deref` trait, to look for matching methods.

For example, given a receiver type like this:

```mermaid
graph LR
  A --Receiver--> B
  A --Deref--> B
  B --Receiver--> C
  B --Deref--> C
  C --Receiver--> D
  D --Receiver-->E
```

it will iterate first through `A<B<C<D<E>>>>` and then `B<C<D<E>>>` and then `C<D<E>>`. At each of those steps, it will look for candidate functions found during the `probe_op` stage.

_Within a given step of iteration_, for instance, just considering a receiver type of `A<B<C<D<E>>>>` with no derefs applied, we might find candidates functions within the `impl` blocks for `A<B<C<D<E>>>>`, `B<C<D<E>>>`, `C<D<E>>`, `D<E>` or `E`. Any of those might have `fn something(self: A<B<C<D<E>>>>>)`.

If we do find multiple candidates in each level of iteration, currently, we show an error about the ambiguity.

The proposed change is:
* switch the error to a warning
* only if this ambiguity results from differently-nested candidates
* pick the most-nested candidate

This all seems counterintuitive, since the _overall iteration_ iterates from the "outer" type via the deref chain. So we always iterate from the outside inwards in terms of dereferencing the receiver. But within each individual iteration step, if there are candidates found in `impl` blocks of differing types, we'll now pick the _innermost_.

Although slightly counterintuitive, the warning should cause everyone to resolve the ambiguity by being explicit, so this should not result in an ongoing cognitive load for Rustaceans. This is a compatible change because previously, errors would always have been shown in this circumstance.

Code example: (pseudo-code)

```rust
// Assume NonNull<T> implements Receiver

struct Dog;

impl Dog {
  fn bark(self: NonNull<Self>) { }
}

fn main() {
   let d: NonNull<Dog> = visit_kennels();
   d.bark(); // obviously calls Dog::bark
}

// Now imagine NonNull adds a bark(self) method in the standard library
// Proposal:
// Compiler CONTINUES to choose Dog::bark but shows a warning along the lines of:
//   Call to Dog::bark shadows NonNull::bark. Be explicit about which method you're aiming to call,
//   e.g. Dog::bark(dog)
```

Overall it appears to make it possible for us to support using `NonNull<T>` and friends as receivers. There are some details to figure out:

* Currently, all by-value methods are searched before autoreffed methods. So, if we had `Dog::bark(self: &NonNull<Self>)` but then added `NonNull::bark(self)` then this would, I think, still cause undesirable shadowing. We may need to rearrange the way `self_pick_all_method` works so it can spot this scenario.
* There are bits of the algorithm around collapsing trait picks, and unstable candidates, which I haven't figured out and we need to see if it fits this proposal.

### Takeaways

The main bits of cunning here are:
* We follow the chain of `Receiver` while identifying candidate methods, but we follow the shorter chain of `Deref` when working out the types to which the actual method receiver can be converted.
* In `pick_all_method`, when we're considering a particular self type `A<B>`, if there's ambiguity about whether to choose a method on `A` or `B` in `A<B>`, we'll choose the method on `B` but show a warning. This allows us to add new methods on types like `NonNull<T>`.
* The error 0282 discussed in the last comment is solved by considering bad types at the end of the `Deref` chain not at the end of the `Receiver` chain.

Thoughts? Does this all make any sense? Can you see any pitfalls?

This is all implemented hackily [here](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/compare/master...adetaylor:rust:receiver_trait_with_target_20231215).

(@madsmtm @Urhengulas!)

comments.author: programmerjake
adetaylor
comments.body: Yep, there's a test equivalent to that and it does pass (but definitely we need more tests before landing all of this, especially to confirm that all this stuff works for trait function resolution too). I also confirmed your test works as expected.

comments.author: joshtriplett
comments.body: @adetaylor First of all, thank you for the *very* detailed explanations and diagrams!

comments.author: joshtriplett
comments.body:
@adetaylor It definitely seems like a potential source of confusion that `Receiver` disambiguates to the *deepest* method in the chain, rather than the *shallowest*, while `Deref` disambiguates to the *shallowest*. (Playground example for `Deref` ambiguity: https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=stable&mode=debug&edition=2021&gist=a47a68ab2aa3683bab64d45c80a42705 ) Leaving aside whether it matches people's intuitions, a few other thoughts: - This gives an example of adding methods on `NonNull`, which will be bypassed with a warning in the case of ambiguity, so adding such methods won't cause user code to stop compiling. What about cases of adding a method on a *deeper* type that effectively shadows the method in the *shallower* type? Could that ever cause an issue? It sounds like probably not, and that's just the price you pay to be a `Receiver` type, but I wanted to ask the question explicitly. - When we give a warning, we should show how to unambiguously call *either* method. Could you give the syntax for each? Are both of those syntaxes clear? The turbofish syntax you're showing seems unintuitive; if I understand correctly, it's explicitly calling the method on `NonNull`, because the method on `Dog` doesn't take a generic parameter of `Dog`? - The help text for the type of `self` is now *very* long: > consider changing to `self`, `&self`, `&mut self`, or a type implementing `Receiver`: for example `self: Box`, `self: Rc`, `self: Arc`, or `self: Pin

` (where P is one of the previous types except `Self` Now that it's a *non-exhaustive* list of examples, I think we should reduce the list to a few representative cases that are easier to list: > consider changing to `self`, `&self`, `&mut self`, or a type implementing `Receiver` such as `self: Box`, `self: Rc`, or `self: Arc`

comments.author: adetaylor
comments.body:
Thanks @joshtriplett !

> @adetaylor It definitely seems like a potential source of confusion that `Receiver` disambiguates to the _deepest_ method in the chain, rather than the _shallowest_, while `Deref` disambiguates to the _shallowest_.

I agree. However,
* The disambiguation of `Receiver` to the deepest method results in a warning. So this situation should not normally persist in people's code. Disambiguation to the shallowest remains the norm in all warning-free Rust code.
* My sense of the [consensus further up this PR](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/3519#issuecomment-1824448078) was: "it seems like there's enough desire for raw pointers, `NotNull`, `Weak` etc. as self types that we should try pretty hard to find a way to avoid compatibility breaks, even if it means we do something a bit unexpected from the user's perspective." I think that's what we're doing here. I think "disambiguate to the deepest in rare circumstances and show a warning until the user makes it explicit" is a good outcome given the strong desires for us to support `NotNull` etc. WDYT?

I agree with your other points and will work on them. In particular, I definitely want to prove that a shoal of turbofishes, or UFCs, can be used to remove all ambiguities.

Incidentally, along with my [overly lengthy comment above](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/3519#issuecomment-1858223400), and the [prototype code](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/compare/master...adetaylor:rust:receiver_trait_with_target_20231215), I have a [PR to change the RFC text correspondingly](https://github.com/adetaylor/rfcs/pull/19) - just in case you were wondering. There's no need to read it - it duplicates the big comment.

comments.author: adetaylor
comments.body:
> * This gives an example of adding methods on `NonNull<T>`, which will be bypassed with a warning in the case of ambiguity, so adding such methods won't cause user code to stop compiling. What about cases of adding a method on a _deeper_ type that effectively shadows the method in the _shallower_ type? Could that ever cause an issue? It sounds like probably not, and that's just the price you pay to be a `Receiver` type, but I wanted to ask the question explicitly.

I think this is unlikely to happen. Supposing we have `NotNull<Giraffe>` and `NotNull` has a pre-existing `eat_leaves(&self)` method. If the giraffe crate adds now adds `impl Giraffe { fn eat_leaves(self: &NotNull<Self>) {} }`, they're explicitly specifying `NotNull` in their new method signature, so they'd have to be very _intentionally_ conflicting with a `NotNull` method.

I can't see a circumstance where that would happen accidentally, _but_ I want to be explicit that I've been thinking of this heavily from the perspective of a `CppRef<T>` type and similar scenarios. Maybe this sort of shadowing could happen when the same primitives are used in a radically different way, but I can't think of a problem.

> * When we give a warning, we should show how to unambiguously call _either_ method. Could you give the syntax for each? Are both of those syntaxes clear? The turbofish syntax you're showing seems unintuitive; if I understand correctly, it's explicitly calling the method on `NonNull<Dog>`, because the method on `Dog` doesn't take a generic parameter of `Dog`?

Yeah, thanks to @riking I realized I should have suggested UFCs here: `Dog::bark(dog)` or `NonNull::bark(dog)`. No new syntax is proposed here. (I've pushed a commit to my [very prototypey branch](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/compare/master...adetaylor:rust:receiver_trait_with_target_20231215) which begins to add the lint.)

> * The help text for the type of `self` is now _very_ long.

Fixed per your suggestion.

comments.author: Nadrieril
comments.body:
> What about cases of adding a method on a deeper type that effectively shadows the method in the shallower type?

This is a mirror of the `Deref` situation, but easier.

For `P<T>: Deref`, the crate that defines `P` mustn't add a `.foo()` inherent method in case a downstream crate A uses `P` on a type that has `foo()`. We can't know what types exist; it's open-world.

For `P<T>: Receiver`, it's the downstream crate A that must be careful to not add a method already defined on `P` as that could break its own downstream crates. That's much easier because we can just look up which methods already exist on `P`; it's (mostly) closed-world.

It's not fully closed-world because versions. To make it fully closed-world, I think we should consider it a breaking change to add methods to a `P: Receiver`.

comments.author: obi1kenobi
comments.body:
> To make it fully closed-world, I think we should consider it a breaking change to add methods to a `P: Receiver`.

This sounds reasonable to me.

I expect we can add a lint to `cargo-semver-checks` for this. I'd be happy to mentor folks toward doing that, or if not, I will sooner or later get around to adding that lint myself.

comments.author: scottmcm
comments.body:
We had a conversation in the triage meeting today.  Here's some thoughts I had, where I'm just speaking for me.  (This is not a team consensus.)

- Any ambiguity resolution that depends on `Deref` concerns me because `Deref` might not be involved, since the type (such as `NonNull`) might be a receiver directly, and thus they're both "direct" inherent methods.
- A new step that looks through the `Deref` chain in the opposite order from the current way that `Deref` is used seems extra-confusing because of that difference.
- I'm unwilling to freeze the set of methods on things like `NonNull` because there's enough methods on it that there's no way there aren't more reasonable ones.  For example, the strict provenance conversations are looking to add some sort of `.addr_strict()` (as a rename of the existing `.addr()`).  If it had one, maybe two, methods then saying "ok, that's it" might be plausible, but there's *49* methods on `*mut T` -- more if you include `*mut [T]` and such.  That's enough that there's no way there won't end up being another one.
- I would like to be able to remove the <https://doc.rust-lang.org/stable/error_codes/E0699.html> error that you get for things like <https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=stable&mode=debug&edition=2021&gist=1bec43f696b546c1fa5480e73b34a3e7>.

I'm more and more thinking that the answer is "well, no, you need a newtype *designed* to be a receiver", rather than allowing the pointer types to be receivers directly -- especially to unblock using it for lots of other types (`Rc`, even `ManuallyDrop`, etc) and we can try to do something else for pointers directly later if really necessary.  At least newtyping is straight-forward and clear, though admittedly would add noise in the code.

comments.author: davidhewitt
comments.body:
> well, no, you need a newtype designed to be a receiver

This got me thinking how I might do that in PyO3. I guess a quick-and-dirty way to build that newtype would be a (trait) method.

```rust
my_smart_pointer.recv().call_method_taking_receiver()
```

And then I wondered: is there a way to reduce that further? Maybe with a new operator to switch name resolution to look only at receiver methods? (I realise almost 100% of the time the answer to a new operator is no.)

```rust
// The idea being that `->` ignores all inherent methods on the 
// smart pointer, and just looks for methods on Receiver::Target
my_smart_pointer->call_method_taking_receiver()
```

I didn't immediately hate seeing `->` in that position, and it's not a million miles away from how `->` is used in C/C++ (although there it works more like `Deref`, I believe). There are probably great reasons not to do this, I thought I'd put it out there for folks to chew on in case it prompted new discussion. Maybe this exact thing has been suggested before anyway.

The interesting thing I saw about using a separate operator to perform arbitrary receiver method lookup is that it can presumably cleanly solve the concerns with ambiguity and breaking changes. Maybe there's a better operator or keyword to use than `->`.

comments.author: PoignardAzur
comments.body:
> It definitely seems like a potential source of confusion that Receiver disambiguates to the deepest method in the chain, rather than the shallowest, while Deref disambiguates to the shallowest. (Playground example for Deref ambiguity: https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=stable&mode=debug&edition=2021&gist=a47a68ab2aa3683bab64d45c80a42705 )

Could this be changed in an edition boundary?

It's not like shadowing methods through Deref is an idiomatic pattern. All Deref implementations I know of are either smart pointers, which tend to avoid having methods, or attempts to emulate object inheritance, in which case shadowing method names is probably a bad idea and should get a warning. (There's also CppRef, but its Deref impl is temporary.)

comments.author: Nadrieril
comments.body:
I do not find this order confusing personally. It resolved to "the most specific method" (i.e. picks a `self: Box<MyType>` impl over a generic `self: Box<T>` over a derefed `self: &MyType`) which is conceptually as it should be imo.

EDIT: well it's not actually that in its current implementation but it's close, see [here](https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=nightly&mode=debug&edition=2021&gist=f310d10f58f72b703b22df7c9ce685ba))

comments.author: nbdd0121
comments.body:
I agree with @Darksonn that the arbitrary self types are orthogonal to raw pointer self types, and I would actually prefer the alternative "Do not enable for pointers" over adding complexity to make pointer self types work.

I also think that the dynamic dispatch is more important than symbol resolution which is the focus of this RFC. I think the dynamic dispatch issue needs to be taken into full account for anything receiver-related to not narrow down future design space. I've written down my evaluation here https://hackmd.io/z4n40072Tqy8MhQPZn0N-g.

comments.author: adetaylor
comments.body: Periodic update, for folks worrying this effort has died: it hasn't! There's been a bit of discussion at recent Rust language team meetings about whether we should do the simple option here (`Receiver` trait with `Target` associated type; supported only for newtype wrappers without methods, only small changes to method resolution logic) vs the complex option (support also for raw pointers, `Weak`, and `NonNull`; more complex method resolution logic to spot shadowing). Current plan is (likely) to go back to the simple version because we don't think it prevents us from doing the more complex version in future. (There are a few side issues I'm looking into, e.g. digging a little deeper into dynamic dispatch).

comments.author: nikomatsakis
comments.body:
@adetaylor, @joshtriplett and I met and discussed this at RustNation. We all aligned on a few points and I wanted to summrize them.

In general, the complexity of method dispatch with distinct roles for `Receiver` and `Deref` seemed too much. We therefore consolidated on the "simple" approach of modifying method dispatch to use `Receiver` in place of `Deref` but without "outer loops" and the rest. This means that, if we permit `*const Self` as a receiver type, adding new methods to `*const` would potentially be breaking -- and we know that T-libs would like to do that -- so we are not recommending that `*const Self` be permitted for the time being.

In short, we are sticking to the existing heuristic

* auto-deref and avoid adding methods where possible, permit user-defined methods (e.g., `Box`, `&`, etc)
* define methods arbitrary, but avoid user-defined methods with that receiver type (raw pointers, `NonNull`)

### How can we support `*const Self` in the future?

If we do want to permit `*const Self` in the future, we have basically two options...

* **YAGNI** -- permit `*const self` but advise users that we will continue to add methods to raw pointer types and therefore `*const self` receivers should be used with caution.
    * **Observation:** The same kind of conflicts can happen today with traits that are implemented for `*const T` receivers, so it is possible to have shadowing, but it is not a big issue in practice.
    * **Observation:** In practice, we have not observed conflicts between methods added to stdlib and libraries to be a major issue -- with the notable exception of one particular case, where handy methods from the ecosystem are moved to stdlib for convenience, which is a peculiar kind of shadowing.
* **Edition** -- Use an edition and create some new method dispatch algorithm that combines deref-and-receiver, perhaps the one that was proposed here, or perhaps some future idea we've not had yet.

### Use cases for methods on raw pointers

Which of the above options is worthwhile really depends on the use cases for raw pointers, so we talked about those. There were three that we came up with, we'd be curious to hear about others...

**Special methods in stdlib, like the `fetch_add` method on `AtomicUsize`.** This is a particular special case, described in detail by pnkfelix in their blog post [How to dismantle an atomic bomb](http://blog.pnkfx.org/blog/2021/03/25/how-to-dismantle-an-atomic-bomb/). The short version is that it would be nice if `fetch_add` and friends took `*const self`. What's worse is that there isn't really an ergonomic way to invoke them without it. We observed that we could simply add these methods as "special cases" if we wanted. It's noteworthy that if we just said "you can use `*const Self`, but we recommend you don't", we could still add these methods, no problem, since they are in the standard library. I think the main thing missing here is that we would want something like "auto_addr_of" to complement today's "auto_ref", because otherwise you have to do something like `addr_of!(x.foo).fetch_add()` which is quite awkward. But that's rather orthogonal.

**Trait dispatch, or the "rayon" use case.** In rayon, we had a `Box<dyn Job>` at some point but realized that using safe reference types was not correct. Therefore the code was refactored to [use a struct, `JobRef`](https://github.com/rayon-rs/rayon/blob/8ccfda38d55e96c5bf50d004f2df95d07468dd4a/rayon-core/src/job.rs#L27-L36) that paired together a fn pointer and the `*const` "self" type. If we could've had something like `*const Self` this would be nicer. **This pattern can be accommodated with this RFC because rayon can easily add a `Job<T>` wrapper.**

### Possible pattern: Raw type in stdlib

One thing we talked about was the possibility of adding a 

```rust
struct Raw<T> {
    pointer: *const T
}
```

type to the standard library, with the intention that people could define "inherent methods" like `fn foo(self: Raw<Self>)` and then "opt-in" to using extension methods by doing `pointer.raw().foo`. This would allow people to explicitly shift between treating the pointer like a pointer versus like a reference to the thing being pointed at. The stdlib would not add inherent methods to `Raw<T>`.

Note though that making `pointer.raw().foo` work again requires some kind of "auto-addr-of", or else people would *actually* have to do something like `addr_of!(pointer).raw().foo()` or perhaps `raw!(foo).foo()`, both of which seem like non-starters.

### My personal conclusion

This section is just my personal conclusions...currently I feel like it makes sense to continue the tradition we have today, where you either have types (like raw) where the focus is on them being a **pointer** or you have types that act like a transparent reference (`&`, `Box`, etc). The former have methods in stdlib and the latter do not. 

I would like to see `*const self` or auto-addr-of or something to permit more ergonomic methods like `fetch_add` and `raw()` but I anticipate most of the usage being either in the stdlib or internal to crates.

I'd like to see more data gathered about conflicts between stdlib and user-provided methods, but I'm less worried, and intrigued by the [idea I raised on Zulip](https://rust-lang.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/213817-t-lang/topic/crate-based.20method.20prioritization) of leveraging the crate graph to help in resolving those kinds of cases.

comments.author: nikomatsakis
comments.body:
@rfcbot fcp merge

Per [my previous comment](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/3519#issuecomment-2046017669), I am moving to **merge** this RFC, and in particular the "simpler" variant of it, in which (effectively) a new trait `Receiver` is added along with a blanket impl of `impl<T: Deref> Receiver for T`, and method dispatch is modified to use `Receiver` instead of `Deref`. Under this proposal, `*const T` does not implement `Receiver` (yet?) and hence is not supported for self-types (without a feature flag, anyway). It can be added later so long as we are willing to go the "YAGNI" route -- adding it later will otherwise require more thought.

comments.author: rfcbot
comments.body:
Team member @nikomatsakis has proposed to merge this. The next step is review by the rest of the tagged team members:

No concerns currently listed.

Once a majority of reviewers approve (and at most 2 approvals are outstanding), this will enter its final comment period. If you spot a major issue that hasn't been raised at any point in this process, please speak up!

comments.author: joshtriplett
comments.body: Signing off on this. And +1 for the approach of warning people writing methods with raw-pointer receivers to accept that there may be (unlikely) breakage due to new methods on raw pointers.

comments.author: scottmcm
comments.body:
+1 to

> In general, the complexity of method dispatch with distinct roles for Receiver and Deref seemed too much.

So I'm in favour of merging the simple step of allowing *user* types to *choose* to allow this, but not allowing it for ordinary raw pointers right now.  I really want to see how the custom-newtype version of this addresses any cases that are talked about with raw pointer receivers today, especially given Niko's post above that the rayon case could do it "easily", before we consider expanding this any further.

@rfcbot reviewed

comments.author: rfcbot
comments.body: :bell: **This is now entering its final comment period**, as per the [review above](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/3519#issuecomment-2046021493). :bell:

comments.author: tmandry
comments.body:
Major props to @adetaylor for not only driving this over the (very long) road to completion, but considering every use case and point of feedback carefully, and being willing to change your approach when needed. I'm impressed by your tenacity and your patience. And a big thank you to everyone else who contributed to the discussion.

I'm happy with the approach we landed on. I think it might make sense to expand support in the future to types which may add methods, like raw pointers. But I also think it makes sense to take it slow, because this is a major addition to Rust.

Let's go!

@rfcbot reviewed

title: add built-in complex number types for C ABI compatibility
body: **Issue by [thestinger](https://github.com/thestinger)** _Friday Nov 15, 2013 at 06:15 GMT_ _For earlier discussion, see https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/10500_ _This issue was labelled with: I-enhancement in the Rust repository_ --- These are sadly not just treated as a simple `struct` on various platforms and have ABI requirements. I think support for the existing C math library is important because this isn't something we want to recreate on every platform.

comments.author: huonw
comments.body: A possible implementation strategy is a new `#[repr]` (e.g. `C_complex`) that can only be attached to a struct of two `f32`s or two `f64`s and forces them to be considered C complex numbers for the purpose of calling `extern "C"` functions.

comments.author: nagisa
comments.body:
As far as I could understand it can and should be represented as an array of 2 floats or doubles to be C99-compatible.

``` rust
struct<T: f32 or f64> Complex<T>{
    data: [T; 2]
}
```

https://gist.github.com/258c1bfdc80970642dd1 seems to work at least on play.rust-lang.org.

comments.author: bluss
comments.body: I don't think playground is using x86, which was the platform with noticeable breakage.

comments.author: main--
comments.body: Looks like [playground is is using x86_64](http://is.gd/dNf4Gs).

comments.author: nagisa
comments.body: Why is x86 behaving differently?

comments.author: bluss
comments.body: @nagisa, see [this comment](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/10500#issue-22715435); we need a new compiler feature for this.

comments.author: nagisa
comments.body: @bluss I saw _how_ it is different. I’m asking _why_ it is different… i.e. is it specified anywhere in the C standard or x86/Sys V ABI (I admit, I have hard time reading and searching these most of the time) or is it just C compilers doing C things?

comments.author: codyps
comments.body:
@nagisa the C standard doesn't specify any calling conventions. SysV ABI for i386 (at least [this version](http://www.uclibc.org/docs/psABI-i386.pdf)) specifies the use of registers for returning `complex float` (but note it also specifies `complex double` is returned in memory)

Not sure if all systems follow that ABI (anyone know what windows does?)

comments.author: nagisa
comments.body:
@jmesmon great! 

To be more precise (for people who have as much trouble searching for things as me) it is table 2.4 on page 14.

It could still be hacked around without (much) language support by doing something along the lines of

``` rust
#[cfg(target_Cabi="SysVx86")] // replace with the relevant target_os and target_arch combinations
#[repr(C)]
struct<T: f32> Complex<T>{ // probably will need to give up parametricity because different representations are necessary for `Complex<f32>` and `Complex<f64>`
    data: i64
}
```

EDIT: it probably wants to be a newtyped alias instead or perhaps an `enum` with `repr(u64)`.

EDIT2: My primary goal is to understand as much as possible about the problem before agreeing or disagreeing with this course of action (adding language level things). This requires at least details on C ABIs used on _all_ platforms we support.

Short summary so far:
- x86 Sys V (pages 12 and 14 of http://www.uclibc.org/docs/psABI-i386.pdf)
  - `Complex<f32>` = u64 
  - `Complex<f64>` = memory = `struct { f64, f64 }` or `[f64; 2]` (?)
- x86_64 Sys V (page 18 of http://www.x86-64.org/documentation/abi.pdf)
  - `Complex<T>` = `struct { T, T}`

comments.author: codyps
comments.body: Shouldn't some form of specialization allow us to keep Complex parameterized?

comments.author: nagisa
comments.body:
VC does not support C `complex.h`. As far as C++ goes… [complex](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/5de6f0bw.aspx) definition.

On x64, the `complex<float>` should be passed the same way as `u64` according to their ABI’s [parameter passing](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/zthk2dkh.aspx).
I gave up searching for x86 conventions.

---

Something to also consider is that once we implement something for `_Complex` at language level, we should also do something similar for `_Decimal`.

title: Unsafe fields
body:
Co-authored by @jswrenn.

Fixes https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/issues/381

[Rendered](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/blob/f5749baed7b7ecfe03d95dc9197d0c1344712f43/text/3458-unsafe-fields.md)

Tracking:

- https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/132922

---

p-FCP comment: https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/3458#issuecomment-2917140820

comments.author: algesten
comments.body:
> By introducing unsafe fields, Rust can improve the situation where a field that is otherwise safe is used as a safety invariant.

I think the motivation could point out _who_ would benefit from this.

I assume it's library authors a making unsafe fields a "reminder to self" about upholding some invariant as opposed to say expecting a unsafe field in an API surface. I.e we still expect `unsafe set_len` rather than `pub unsafe len`, right?

comments.author: programmerjake
comments.body: this seems closely related to `mut(self)` fields https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/3323 which should probably be mentioned.

comments.author: jhpratt
comments.body:
> I assume it's library authors a making unsafe fields a "reminder to self" about upholding some invariant as opposed to say expecting a unsafe field in an API surface. I.e we still expect `unsafe set_len` rather than `pub unsafe len`, right?

Not necessarily. It is entirely reasonable that `Vec.len` _could_ be exposed. Whether it actually is exposed is a decision solely for T-libs-api. Likewise with the inner fields of the various nonzero types. I'd rather not tie down who this is intended for, as it truly is intended for everyone. I know I've written a binary that had fields relied upon in unsafe code — there was just no way to make it actually unsafe.



> this seems closely related to `mut(self)` fields #3323 which should probably be mentioned.

Related, sure, but beyond the mention in unresolved questions, I'm not sure how it could be mentioned. Pretty much the only overlap is what's considered a "mutable access", which I didn't feel necessary to be copy-pasted.

comments.author: jsgf
jhpratt
comments.body: @jsgf Great question. I don't have an immediate answer, though I believe the mechanism currently in the compiler would require the entire initializer to be unsafe.

comments.author: mo8it
djc
comments.body: The implicit notion that only mutation is unsafe (and reading is not) seems tricky. Do you have reasoning to prove that we'll never need fields that are unsafe to read? Maybe there should be an alternative syntax proposal (like `unsafe(mut)` or `mut(unsafe)`) that makes this more obvious/explicit?

comments.author: idanarye
comments.body: @mo8it I don't want to spam the comments here with a discussion about typed builder, so I've opened a discussion in my repository instead: https://github.com/idanarye/rust-typed-builder/discussions/103

comments.author: idanarye
comments.body:
Regarding the RFC itself - I think you are trying to solve a visibility issue with the safety mechanism, which is the wrong tool for the job. You gave `Vec` as an example, and it looks like you want to grant public read access to its `len` field (so that you don't have to use `len()` as a method? Let's ignore the question if that's big enough an improvement to justify such a feature). To do so, you are willing to provide public write access to it as well but make that access `unsafe`.

But why?

I mean, it's obvious why you don't want to give regular write access to `len`. But why give any access at all? Even if you require an `unsafe` block, what good will come from letting external users modify the `len` field without going through a method that upholds the invariant? As long as we are devising a brand new feature, wouldn't it make more sense to add a feature that gives public read access without _any_ write access _at all_ (other than private access from inside the defining module, of course)?

I'm aware of the [`set_len`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/vec/struct.Vec.html#method.set_len) method that grants such access, but this is an explicit decision to give such access, with a fully documented method. Not a side-effect of wanting to provide a non-method-call read access to the field.

------

Another thing - conceptually it never makes sense to define only one field as `unsafe`. The invariant is a property of the struct as a whole. If this is unsafe:

```rust
let mut vec = Vec::new();
vec.len = 4; // UNSAFE!!!
```
The why not this?
```rust
let mut vec = Vec::from([1, 2, 3, 4]);
vec.buf = RawVec::new(); // perfectly safe apparently
```

Yes, `buf` is not publicly exposed at all, but inside the module `len` will need `unsafe` block to modify and `buf` won't, even though the invariant is about both of them, together, and how they interact with each other.

Whatever the semantics of unsafe fields will be - conceptually it makes more sense to put the `unsafe` on the struct itself. If there are fields that are not part of the invariant, they should not be part of the struct - because unsafety should be contained as much as possible and not contaminated with unrelated data. The only reason putting `unsafe` only on `len` seems to make case in your example is that the goal - as I've said before - is about visibility, not about safety.

comments.author: burdges
jhpratt
comments.body:
> Do you have reasoning to prove that we'll never need fields that are unsafe to read?

How could a field of a struct be unsafe to read?

---

> You gave `Vec` as an example, and it looks like you want to grant public read access to its `len` field (so that you don't have to use `len()` as a method?

Within the module it's defined in (as the field is private), it is currently _safe_ to assign any value, despite the fact that it can lead to undefined behavior. Said another way, _the current behavior is inherently unsound_.

Nothing in the RFC so much as hints at `Vec.len` being made public, nor is an RFC an appropriate place to make a change like that. It is an example of a field that should be unsafe to avoid unsoundness and nothing more.

> Another thing - conceptually it never makes sense to define only one field as unsafe. 

I never claimed that was the case.

> The why not this?
> 
> ```rust
> let mut vec = Vec::from([1, 2, 3, 4]);
> vec.buf = RawVec::new(); // perfectly safe apparently
> ```

Inclusion of one example does not mean that everything not included is forbidden. There is simply no reason to repeat the same thing for every field. Of course `buf` would also be unsafe. I used `Vec.len` as the example because it's a clear, obvious example where its safety invariants are publicly documented.

> The invariant is a property of the struct as a whole.

> If there are fields that are not part of the invariant, they should not be part of the struct

For `Vec`, yes, but only because all fields of the strict interact with all other fields.

I have [_real world_ code](https://github.com/time-rs/time/blob/cbf134922d54207e23096d0a561341ec39fc931f/time/src/parsing/parsed.rs#L113-L158) where this is not the case. The `flags` field is for whether other fields are initialized. Note that some fields have niche value optimization, and as such don't interact with other fields in any way. Are you asserting that `month: Option<Month>` and other similar fields should be in a separate struct solely because it has niche value optimization? That appears to a logical conclusion as a result of what you've said.

---

> `static mut`s require unsafe blocks for both writing and reading. An unsafe field would likely be some similar construction, so unsafe for both writing and reading.

`static mut` requires unsafe due to inherent data races between threads. Unsafe fields have no such issue.

> Ain't clear this proposal handles auto-traits correctly, even if some use case exists.

I don't follow. What problems do you see with handling auto traits? The type of the field is unchanged, so there would be no impact on auto traits.

comments.author: scottmcm
comments.body:
(@jhpratt I'd love for you to steal something like the text in this post for the RFC)

> I assume it's library authors a making unsafe fields a "reminder to self" about upholding some invariant as opposed to say expecting a unsafe field in an API surface.

A huge incentive for it, to me at least, is helping avoid misunderstandings about the model.

For example, a 2020 PACMPL paper contains the following statement:

> To check how prevalent unsafe is used as a documentation feature, our queries gathered data for unsafe traits and unsafe functions with safe implementations.

`set_len` being an `unsafe fn` is of course not merely a "documentation feature".  It's an absolutely critical part of the soundness of `Vec`.  (And the paper does talk about "invariants that are *potentially* critical for upholding Rust’s safety guarantees" (emphasis added), so I don't think their analysis is incorrect, but I still find the phrasing curious.)

Thus a huge win of this would be to avoid the (from the same paper)

> After all, the compiler does not force developers to declare such functions as `unsafe` -- in contrast to other unsafe features.

Having the body of `set_len` *do something the compiler recognizes as unsafe* is a big help to people understanding the soundness of `Vec`, and in general to people adding new features inside existing unsafety privacy boundaries.

Especially combined with other accepted work like <https://rust-lang.github.io/rfcs/2316-safe-unsafe-trait-methods.html> we could start even doing things like clippy lints for "why is this unsafe when it doesn't do anything unsafe?  Should one of the types involved be marked unsafe?"

If we don't have to link to [tootsie pops](http://smallcultfollowing.com/babysteps/blog/2016/05/27/the-tootsie-pop-model-for-unsafe-code/#tweaking-the-concept-of-a-boundary) as often because changing things that are relied on by `unsafe` code is itself `unsafe`, I'd consider that a big win.

---

As another way to look at this, it's weird that when I'm writing a method on by type with a safety invariant that I can do `Self { a, b }` and it's totally "safe", whereas if I call `Self::new_unchecked(a, b)` I need an unsafe block and tidy nags me to write a safety comment.

Tidy should nag about a safety comment for the constructor too, so that I'm not disincentivized to use the other, correctly-marked-`unsafe` function when writing things.

comments.author: burdges
comments.body:
> If the invariant is violated, the result is undefined behavior.

> That is a significant argument in favor of something better.

This RFC is not better because memory safety also helps when writing unsafe code.

The `unsafe` keyword is not simply a marker to tell you where to read more carefully.  Its a marker of where safety rules must be violated. 

In other words, we always convert regular code invariants into memory safety assurance, but these regular code invariants have exactly the same risks as other regular code, including their own memory safety concerns.  This RFC confuses the memory safety consumed in maintaining the regular code invariant with the actually unsafe options the code requires.

In the past, `unsafe fn` bodies were unsafe blocks, but rust changed this to reduce the unsafe code surface area.  This RFC is a mistake because it increases the unsafe code surface area with no benefits, given the same cautions can be maintained in other ways, like by variable naming, etc.

`UnsafeInvariant` not being used is evidence this feature is not required.  `UnsafeInvariant` would make sense if you wanted to split the regular code invariant across distant visibility boundaries.  In practice, `unsafe fn`s always sufficed, or indeed proved more nuanced than `UnsafeInvariant`.

Anyways..

I think this discussion belongs in https://github.com/rust-lang/unsafe-code-guidelines where at least some people think formally about the unsafe code boundary.

comments.author: jhpratt
comments.body:
> In other words, we always convert regular code invariants into memory safety assurance, but these regular code invariants have exactly the same risks as other regular code, including their own memory safety concerns. This RFC confuses the memory safety consumed in maintaining the regular code invariant with the actually unsafe options the code requires.

This is your fundamental misunderstanding.

Other code in `Vec` relies on the invariants of `Vec.len` in ways that leads to undefined behavior if the invariants are broken. Fields like `Vec.len` are not "regular code invariants" — they are tightly coupled to whether the code is sound or not. It is a _soundness invariant_. You cannot possibly claim otherwise.

> given the same cautions can be maintained in other ways, like by variable naming, etc.

Frankly, it's thinking like this that led to the creation of Rust. Thread safety can be maintained if everyone is super careful, but we all know how that works out. Likewise with a million other things. Programmers _can not be relied upon_ to do the right thing. We have to force them to do it by leveraging the compiler wherever possible.

> I think this discussion belongs in https://github.com/rust-lang/unsafe-code-guidelines where at least some people think formally about the unsafe code boundary.

I have no idea why you think the discussion belongs there, particularly as you're the one that initiated it here.

comments.author: burdges

Yes, but this does not make altering `Vec.len` within the `Vec` module an unsafe operation.  That's not how safety works.

What happens if I've unsafe code which relies upon an invariant between the values in a slice, so your unsafe field is a `&[*mut Foo]` or `&[usize]`?  I want memory safety around the regular code invariants in how I maintain this slice.  Yes, those regular code invariants control memory safety in how the module get used, hence privacy. No.  There is a formal model from the rustbelt project about how unsafe works, which gets discussed in the unsafe code guidlines repo.  We should only expand what falls under unsafe code if the formal model says so.  This RFC confuses those really important formal models with mere "read this" markers.

pub struct Vec<T> { // ignoring the allocator for brevity

}

comments.author: RalfJung
comments.body: IMO the annotation on `len` makes a lot more sense. The type `usize` for `len` is a plain lie: this cannot just be an arbitrary integer! If we had refinement types, we would write `len: usize @ length_of(buf)`, or so, but we don't, so we propose to write `unsafe len: usize` instead to clearly document that `usize` here is incomplete information.

comments.author: idanarye
comments.body: @RalfJung What does `length_of(buf)` even mean? This value is not known at compile time, and may even change at runtime after setting `len`.

comments.author: RalfJung
comments.body:
Yes, refinement types don't have to be compile-time constants. It is meant to be pseudo-notation to refer to the length of the initialized part of the buffer.

The point is that `len` is *not* a perfectly safe field. It has a subtle invariant. In this particular case the invariant is relational (it also involves `buf`), but that shouldn't distract from `len` being unsafe. (Of course `buf` should also be unsafe.)

comments.author: soqb
comments.body:
i think the distinction between `unsafe struct Even { num: u32 }` and `struct Even { unsafe num: u32 }` is pretty insignificant.

both clearly represent "here are some fields which are unsafe to mutate".

i think i would prefer if we didn't implement both since they have very strong overlap.

unsafe fields have the benefit that we get finer-grained control when maintaining the invariants of types without sacrificing layout guarantees and brevity by separating fields into more types. however, it is true that plenty of safety invariants rely on the relationship between fields rather than the values of individual fields (e.g. `Vec::{len, buf}`) so unsafe types, in their shotgun approach to safety, guarantee that all fields that could be unsafe are marked as such.

imo, unsafe types are cause for concern in terms of code accessibility. if a new Rust user sees `unsafe struct ArenaAllocator` they might assume that the type requires arcane knowledge of unsafe and be deterred from using the type, whereas `pub(self) unsafe field arena_size: usize` makes it much more obvious that the unsafety is carefully kept in check by the library. whether or not this assumption is reasonable is another question, but to me it seems plausible.

we might also want to reserve the `unsafe struct Foo` syntax for other language features (for instance, types that can only be named within an unsafe context) which could be useful for compile-time programming.

struct Foo {

#[allow(safe_fields_adjacent_to_unsafe)]

}

comments.author: RalfJung
comments.body:
> @RalfJung Wouldn't a `dyn` trait raw pointer already be unsafe to use, just like any other raw pointer?

Please read the linked issue(s). :)

Raw pointers are unsafe to read and write, but they are safe to cast to different raw pointers (e.g. from `*const dyn Trait` to `*const dyn Supertrait`), and it is safe to call raw-pointer-receiver methods on them. The latter two operations require the vtable to be valid. The actual pointer can still be arbitrary, including null.

comments.author: jhpratt
comments.body: How would the act of reading the field be any different than, say, having a locally created variable that does the same thing? It feels like the safety invariant is being placed in the wrong location with what you're saying.

comments.author: RalfJung
comments.body: Unsafe fields would be a way to get some compiler help that makes this less dangerous than just using a local variable. One could write a `DanglingVtableRawPtr` type or so to track the fact that this raw pointer does not satisfy the usual safety invariant. However such a type can only be written if we have custom datatype invariants that *weaken* the usual safety invariant of the underlying type. Which in turns means that in the context of that type, even reading the unsafe field is dangerous.

comments.author: idanarye
comments.body:
> Raw pointers are unsafe to read and write, but they are safe to cast to different raw pointers (e.g. from `*const dyn Trait` to `*const dyn Supertrait`), and it is safe to call raw-pointer-receiver methods on them. The latter two operations require the vtable to be valid. The actual pointer can still be arbitrary, including null.

If the compiler guarantees that the vtable is valid even for raw pointers, then there is no problem. If it does not, then these two operations must be unsafe even without using unsafe fields. If you want to allow invalid vtables for raw pointers and still have these operations be safe, then you are violating one of Rust's core tenants by allowing UB in safe code - even if the unsafe fields feature never gets implemented.

comments.author: RalfJung
RalfJung
comments.body:
> I guess what I'm trying to say is that reading an unsafe field should never be any different than reading a local variable. The only difference is whether the field belongs to a struct, which I can't envision as having any impact on anything.
> 
> If it's unsafe to do this with a field, how is it not unsafe to do it with a local variable as well? And if it is unsafe in for local variables, why is the unsafety not being enforced in a manner that would include locals?

The RFC is for unsafe fields, not unsafe local variables. It is completely possible to have a local variable where writing the wrong value to that variable can cause UB -- just like writing the wrong value to `vec.len` can cause UB. This RFC doesn't attempt to change anything about the local variable situation. Therefore I don't think it is a valid argument to try and draw a parallel between local variables and fields here.

"Reading from unsafe fields is safe" is based on the argument that the unsafe field can only ever have *stricter* requirements than its type indicates. E.g., an integer can be required to be even. And in general that is true. But it is certainly possible that someone might have a field with an invariant that is deliberately weaker than its safety invariant (say, an `&str` that is not UTF-8), and then carefully only do things with that field that are still valid. (Of course the field must satisfy the validity invariant or else we'd have UB.) In those situations, one has to be careful even just reading that data.

imo, we should make mutable access to unsafe fields unsafe but not immutable access and the vtable argument does not change my mind.

the UB in that example is not caused by the reading the unsafe field with the vtable pointer, it is caused by writing an invalid value to the field (which would be an unsafe operation) and then passing it uncritically to a method (which should be unsafe) that reads the pointer (an unsafe operation).

the only context in which immutable access to a field can *cause* UB is when it is read unwittingly while intentionally put in an invalid state by code could be marked unsafe by this feature, which feels more like a privacy/documentation concern.

comments.author: idanarye
comments.body:
> We can totally allow invalid vtables and have the casts be safe as long as we ensure invalid vtables never make it to unknown safe code.

That's not how things work anywhere else in Rust. The idiomatic way to deal with unsafety in Rust is to have some underlying type with unsafe operation that can only be done in `unsafe` blocks, and a safe wrapper that maintains the safety invariants and exposes a safe API that can be used without `unsafe` blocks. But the potentially UBing operations on their underlying unsafe types are always `unsafe` - Rust does not make them safe and just [ensures these values never make it to unknown safe code](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rp8hvyjZWHs).

comments.author: RalfJung
comments.body:
@jhpratt 
> I'm well aware of what the RFC says; I wrote it.

I know. :)

> Frankly, I don't buy that. Let's use &str not being UTF-8 as the example, as that's easier to wrap my head around. The documentation couldn't be clearer:

Indeed we cannot call any `str` method on that `&str`. But maybe we know that the string only ha a single place where it's non-UTF-8 and we are sure this will be patched back up before calling an `&str` operation. Then this is all safe and sound.

Now, for `&str` it is a bit silly, since really you should be using `&[u8]` then, to have the types document explicitly that this might or might not be valid UTF-8. Basically there is general guidance that the type you give to the field should be an *underapproximation* of the actual invariant satisfied by the field. We should always try to do that (and we've had soundness bugs due to violations of this principle).

But what if there is no other type one could switch to? That's why I brought up raw pointers.

@idanarye
I am very familiar with the idiomatic way to encapsulate unsafety in Rust. I literally wrote a PhD on that topic. However, *inside* one of these abstractions, it is common for unsafe code to temporarily break some invariants, and carefully ensure that all invariants are restored before safe code takes control again. So yes this is very much how things are frequently done in Rust.

For raw pointers specifically, it is common for code to need some kind of sentinel value, so I can totally see unsafe code dealing with raw pointers that don't have a valid vtable -- and carefully ensuring that we never use them in the wrong way.

comments.author: soqb
comments.body:
> Indeed we cannot call any str method on that &str. But maybe we know that the string only ha a single place where it's non-UTF-8 and we are sure this will be patched back up before calling an &str operation. Then this is all safe and sound.

that's not the case. it's unsafe to forge a `&str` for any bytes that are non-utf8 and that unsafe code is making an irrevocable promise to the compiler. the internal representation of `str` or the machine code for either the method you use to put the invalid bytes into a str, or to remove them again could change in some imperceptible way that exhibits unsoundness because they are free to rely absolutely on the fact that str must contain utf-8 bytes.

a more transparent example is calling `NonZeroFoo::new_unchecked(0)` only to immediately set the value to `1`. even though this will almost certainly compile away, it's not guaranteed and so it is unsound and cannot be relied upon.

from the perspective of Rust, there is no difference between any two sets of invariants on unsafe apis. it is not the case that some must be upheld and others are negotiable (as you suggest with the byte repr of `str`). they all must be upheld totally and at all times.

(i'm not sure whether this actually applies at the intra-crate level or only between crates since within a single crate we have total control over the code, likewise for pinned dependencies)

comments.author: withoutboats
comments.body:
(NOT A CONTRIBUTION)

I'm really in favor of adding unsafe fields. I want to try again to explain the relationship between unsafe fields and "the unsafe boundary is the module;" because I think its important to understand how this is an improvement on the current state of Rust.

When Ralf wrote that the unsafe boundary is the module, what he meant was that the correctness of unsafe code depended on the safe code around it, and further that that we could only validate that that safe code was correct because privacy meant that safe code had to exist in the same module. But this is not a universal truth! This is simply because (e.g.) the unsafe code that is involved in defining `Vec` depends on the invariant that at least the first `len` elements of its `buf` are initialized.

Today, we have no way to enforce that invariant but with module privacy and hoping we catch any violation of that invariant in the same module. The point of this RFC is to make modifying `len` and `buf` unsafe, so that the compiler flags writes to those fields as requiring an unsafe block and therefore requiring special attention to ensure you've upheld the invariant. Now you don't need to trust yourself that you've caught all the potential violations in your module: the compiler tracks them for you.

And as a result, you *could* stop depending on privacy at all in enforcing your invariants, though that doesn't mean you *should* make those fields public. You still may want them all to be private for all the other good reasons, but either way you're in a strictly better place.

A similar example is to shift negative reasoning in the trait system (which can be necessary for soundness) from relying on the orphan rules to relying on explicit negative impls.

---

> IMO the annotation on len makes a lot more sense.

Clearly both fields need to be unsafe, because the invariant is a relation between the two fields that can be violated by mutating either of them. You also want to be flagged when you reassign `buf` to confirm that you have reset `len` to a correct length.

---

I think unsafe reads are far less common than unsafe writes, but that the RFC should at least be forward compatible with them. I do think there's a clear connection to the restrictions RFC here - both visibility and safety are facts about fields that can be modified either for only writability, for only readability, or for both, and they should have aligned syntax to make this system clearer.
