comments.author: clarfonthey
comments.body:
Maybe this RFC needs the concept of unsafe references instead of just unsafe fields?

Right now, unsafe references are just... pointers, but it would be useful to have the concept of a reference with a proper lifetime that just requires an unsafe block to use. This way, you minimise what's needed to use it.

The issue is what syntax should be used for the fields, since as mentioned, interior mutability means that simply requiring unsafe for mutable references is not enough. But, many methods would probably prefer offering safe reads and unsafe writes.

Maybe instead, you could require methods that take the unsafe references and return safe ones? That way, you can still make reads "mostly" safe, while not having to have a syntax to describe whether immutable access is safe but mutable access is not.

To add onto here, one motivating use case is methods like those on `OsString` that give a mutable reference to an underlying vector: since these are methods, not fields, they aren't covered by the RFC, but they should still be considered unsafe.

comments.author: jhpratt
comments.body:
Unsafe references wouldn't cover a huge swath of situations where a field has an invariant. `Vec::len` and `Vec::buf` should both be unsafe, and only having unsafe references wouldn't handle that situation at all. Likewise with integers inside the standard library that are required to be within a specific range (for optimization purposes).

I understand that there is some level of desire for unsafe reads and am open to suggestions on syntax. I would _vastly_ prefer the shorter syntax to be for only writes being unsafe, as it's hard to imagine that not being the far more common scenario. Note that as stated above, even a `!Freeze` bound is not sufficient, so handling it in a user-exposable way may not be trivial.

comments.author: clarfonthey
comments.body:
So, I should probably clarify what I mean by unsafe references: the idea is that any reference taken from an unsafe field is automatically unsafe, meaning that `&self.len` for example in `Vec` would be coerced to e.g. `&unsafe usize` and `&mut self.len` would be coerced to `&unsafe mut usize` or something like that. Unsafe references require an unsafe block to deref, either automatically or explicitly.

However, by adding the option to make references themselves unsafe, you could make a method, for example, that returns an unsafe reference. Probably just allowing coercion or casting to unsafe references is enough, but it would cover the case, where, for example, `OsString` offers an equivalent to this:

```rust
pub(crate) fn as_mut_vec(&mut self) -> &mut Vec<u8>;
```

which could, in effect, return this instead:

```rust
pub(crate) fn as_mut_vec(&mut self) -> &unsafe mut Vec<u8>;
```

Since even though the fields inside the struct can be marked as unsafe, and you can make the method *obtaining* a mutable reference unsafe, it effectively still overrides the safety mechanisms if you can still use the resulting reference safely. (Or, for example, you'd have to make an explicit wrapper somehow.)

Like I said, it's possible to exploit this by using pointers, which always require an unsafe block to deref, but this removes the extra lifetime checks by the compiler and makes it much easier to write unsound code. I don't think that we need to reintroduce the possibility of data races and other bad things just to help ensure that people follow invariants correctly.

comments.author: burdges
comments.body:
> Vec::len and Vec::buf should both be unsafe

This claim still seems unjustified.

We always have field invariants in safe code which if violated break soundness.  If some field is only visible in one module, then 
1. that field cannot be unsafe because the module boundary has either safely encapsulate the unsafety (or else the module is unsound). 
2. marking that field unsafe would create "unsafe creap" where unsafe blocks get used unecessarily, and inadvertently permit more real unsafe things, which shall eventually worsens supply chain attacks in particular.

We might've uses for `unsafe pub(..)` fields whenever one module cannot safely encapsulate that field, so then other modules must express the unsafety when accessing the field.  Yet, shouldn't there _always_ exist one module within which accessing a field is safe?

Afaik Vec::len looks completely encapsulated from the public interface.  Yes, this includes an unsafe setter method.  Is the concern really the internal interfaces?  Is the argument here that the vec module consistes of several modules which share access to `Vec::len`?   Or is the argument here that the eixstance of this setter method changes things somehow? 

It's clear the current unsafety rules for the module/crate boundary makes sense.  It's unclear why this change really makes sense though, maybe this change is just not being explained well enough, but something here is really missing.

comments.author: digama0
comments.body:
> We always have field invariants in safe code which if violated break soundness.

No, we don't *always* have such invariants. Many modules get by just fine without any internal unsafety. Vec is one of those types that contains internal unsafety, and there is a very formalistic reason why Vec::len is an unsafe field: the type's (module-defined, via documentation) safety invariant includes a property that links this field and Vec::buf, meaning that it is unsafe to make modifications to one field independently of the other. This is exactly the reason why `Vec::set_len` is unsafe, and the reasoning applies equally to the field itself, whether it is public or not.

> Afaik Vec::len looks completely encapsulated from the public interface. Yes, this includes an unsafe setter method. Is the concern really the internal interfaces?

Yes, this is about the internal interfaces. It is a very uncomfortable situation that `Vec::set_len` is an unsafe method which performs no unsafe operations; one could imagine a linter that complains that this is an unnecessary use of `unsafe`, and the compiler would not be able to realize at all if you just dropped it, but of course this is unsound. Unsafe fields would ensure that such a linter would not have any false positives of this form - the unsafety would be traceable back to the unsafe fields, and the unsafe fields would be traceable back to the decision to add a safety invariant to `Vec` above and beyond what the contained types alone provide.

It's also not just about internal interfaces, in the sense that if Vec had an unsafe field then it could plausibly make it public, which sometimes helps in borrow checking situations. This is unlikely to happen for Vec itself because of stability matters, but similar types in downstream libraries would be able to safely expose their fields without unsoundness, even if they have nontrivial safety invariants.

comments.author: ssokolow
comments.body:
> Yes, this is about the internal interfaces. It is a very uncomfortable situation that `Vec::set_len` is an unsafe method which performs no unsafe operations; one could imagine a linter that complains that this is an unnecessary use of `unsafe`, and the compiler would not be able to realize at all if you just dropped it, but of course this is unsound. Unsafe fields would ensure that such a linter would not have any false positives of this form - the unsafety would be traceable back to the unsafe fields, and the unsafe fields would be traceable back to the decision to add a safety invariant to `Vec` above and beyond what the contained types alone provide.

To me, this argument feels flawed because it seems like any "a linter might think it doesn't need to be unsafe" argument would apply equally to something where the "unsafe field" is on the other side of an FFI or RPC boundary where Rust can't do anything about it.

(In a sense, it feels similar to flawed arguments I've seen people make about thread-safety because they don't understand that opaque things beyond Rust's control such as platform APIs might have a "must be called from the init-ing thread" invariant... for example, because they contain use of thread-local storage.)

comments.body: Not really, because any FFI boundary already involves an `unsafe { ... }` block. The trouble is only when it's a field in a plain struct, since rustc thinks it understands the situation here and we have to explicitly instruct it that no it doesn't, there is something more subtle going on and please mark anything that touches this field for careful review.

comments.author: ssokolow
comments.body:
Note that I said "or RPC". You don't need `unsafe` to do socket I/O.

**EDIT:** Fundamentally, my point is that this argument feels like it's failing to take into account the breadth of real-world situations which are superficially similar, but contain some small detail which make this proposed solution non-viable.

comments.author: digama0
comments.body:
For platform APIs, it's the responsibility of the library author to mark the functions as safe or unsafe, and in particular to only mark them as safe when they really can't be used to cause memory unsafety. The code behind these APIs always bottoms out at some FFI or other already-unsafe-for-language-reasons code, so there is an `unsafe { ... }` block somewhere between the safe interface and the language primitive where you can hang some documentation.

In this situation, by contrast, there is no "code behind the API" because it is a language primitive - a field access - so the language itself has to ensure that it is sufficiently conservative as to not allow safe use of things that are actually unsafe. And since this is not something the compiler can determine, we need syntax to inform it to mark a given field access as unsafe.

comments.author: ssokolow
comments.body:
I don't dispute that. What I dispute is that it's useful to talk about what is, in effect, a kind of effect system (caring about unsafe methods which perform no machine-recognizably unsafe operations) as a viable retrofit at this point in Rust's lifecycle.

It reminds me of how Rust used to have an effect system in the pre-v1.0 days and it was removed because people weren't using it enough to justify it being part of the language.

comments.author: digama0
comments.body:
> What I dispute is that it's useful to talk about what is, in effect, a kind of effect system (caring about unsafe methods which perform no machine-recognizably unsafe operations) as a viable retrofit at this point in Rust's lifecycle.

This is not really a retrofit at all, this is the way rust already works. The distinction between safe and unsafe is not anything machine-recognizable, it is a system of predicates associated to types, not necessarily decidable or otherwise machine-checkable, which together ensure no UB but in general is an underapproximation of this. The fact that the safety invariants on types and functions is user-defined is a core tenet of this scheme, and it has been in use in rust library design since the very beginning. See [RustBelt](https://plv.mpi-sws.org/rustbelt/popl18/) for a formalization of this concept, or this [blog post](https://www.ralfj.de/blog/2018/08/22/two-kinds-of-invariants.html), in particular the "Custom Safety Invariants" section.

From this perspective, unsafe fields are fixing an existing hole in being able to do local reasoning about soundness. This hole is not catastrophic because we have a back-up plan, the module boundary, which gives another (less local) mechanism for reasoning about soundness, but it would be useful nonetheless to have this locality property so that we only have to audit unsafe blocks instead of having to audit all code, safe or unsafe, in any module containing a type with custom safety invariants.

comments.author: ssokolow
comments.body:
And my argument is that, for such a change to be something one can rely on, it requires a major shift that I'm not sure is viable at the point in the ecosystem's lifecycle.

Again, I'm not arguing for or against unsafe fields. I'm saying that this passage cannot be an argument for `unsafe` fields because, given the state of the ecosystem, it can only reasonably exist as an opt-in Clippy lint, and Rust has a history of letting added syntax stand on its own merits, independent of what lints it would enable.

> It is a very uncomfortable situation that `Vec::set_len` is an unsafe method which performs no unsafe operations; one could imagine a linter that complains that this is an unnecessary use of `unsafe`, and the compiler would not be able to realize at all if you just dropped it, but of course this is unsound. Unsafe fields would ensure that such a linter would not have any false positives of this form - the unsafety would be traceable back to the unsafe fields, and the unsafe fields would be traceable back to the decision to add a safety invariant to `Vec` above and beyond what the contained types alone provide.

In other words, if `unsafe` fields can't justify themselves on their own merits, then your hypothetical is a bug in the linter for introducing such a footgun, not a bug in the language for failing to introduce new syntax.

comments.author: RalfJung
comments.body:
@digama0 has given the arguments they justify unsafe fields on their own merits: they help ensure correctness of unsafe code. That "linter" may as well be the programmer themselves, trying to figure out what is unsafe an where, placing SAFETY comments. Without unsafe fields this is non-trivial to do correctly once there are type-level safety invariants (which is a very common pattern); with unsafe fields the compiler can help. This is something where the library author can opt-in on a per-type basis, so the fact that there already is a lot of code out there is not a detriment to this argument.

Unsafe fields have been talked about, and used in discussions around unsafe, since ~forever. They are a well-established concept at this point, the RFC "just" makes the compiler finally catch up.

> That "linter" may as well be the programmer themselves, trying to figure out what is unsafe an where, placing SAFETY comments. 

Alright, but adding unsafe blocks incurs some cost:  You must check that only the desired `unsafe fn`s get invoked.  If some type has both `Vec` and an unsafe field, then you've lost any extra warnings when calling `Vec::set_let` while accessing the unsafe field.

If the linter is the programmer, then it's more constrained to simply append `_unsafe` to the field name.  This avoids "unsafe creep" where more code becomes unsafe, while exposing when unsafe fields get accessed.

comments.author: RalfJung
comments.body:
> If the linter is the programmer, then it's more constrained to simply append _unsafe to the field name. This avoids "unsafe creep" where more code becomes unsafe, while exposing when unsafe fields get accessed.

That's very un-Rusty. It's not how we handle other unsafe operations.

Having more than one unsafe op in an unsafe block is a potential issue, but it is pre-existing so I don't think that should prevent us from more properly tracking unsafety where we can.

comments.author: burdges
comments.body:
As you're replying by email, I'll un-edit my above comment and paste it here.

There exist alternatives that avoid "unsafe creap" while still doing enforcement in rustc:

_Idea 1._  Explicit rolls

An unsafe field can only be written from special `unsafe(TypeName) { .. }` blocks, but these "roll focused" unsafe blocks disallow regular unsafe operations.  A regular `unsafe { .. }` block cannot directly write to unsafe fields.

We'd permit `unsafe(TypeName) fn` too, even if `TypeName` is a ZST or lacks unsafe fields, so `unsafe fn`s can be roll focused too, with their rolls again being multuly exclusive to regular unsafe  blocks.

In future, we could add `unsafe(TypeName1,TypeName2,..,TypeNamen,number) { .. }` blocks that permit exactly `number` regular unsafe operations, and any number of roll focused unsafe operations for `TypeName1,TypeName2,..,TypeNamen`.

In this, `TypeName` is the identifier after `struct` or `union`, not a fully qualified type.  Alternatively, rolls could be specified like `unsafe(module) { .. }`  where `module` is the module where `TypeName` gets declared.

In this way, you can mark as much code as you like `unsafe` for rolls you create, without fearing that regular `unsafe fn`s get invoked more often. 

_Idea 2._  Implicit rolls

If people dislike the verbosity of `unsafe(TypeName)` then rustc could simply create an "unsafe report" that infers & documents rolls:
```
module_path::fn_name1 : 
  unsafe block 1 : 2 primitive unsafe operations

module_path::fn_name2 : 
  unsafe block 1 : field writes to TypeName
  unsafe block 2 : std::vec::Vec::set_len
```
You could lint against unsafe blocks having multiple inferred rolls too.

comments.author: RalfJung
comments.body: That sounds quite similar to ideas around more explicitly discharging proof obligations, which have been floating around for a while. As I already said in my previous message: this is not a new problem. Calling more than one unsafe fn in an unsafe block happens regularly. I don't think unsafe fields make this so much worse that we have to block them on a more fine-grained unsafety tracking mechanism.

comments.author: kennytm
CodesInChaos
jhpratt
comments.body:
So…I'm conflicted. Yes, initialization is included, but it is already known that the data contained in the struct is valid. The situation that is motivation for this RFC is when a field has its own invariant and/or relies on another field in the struct. In my head, I view the `unsafe` bit being unchecked assignment to a field. _But_ `Clone` and `Copy` are not that, as the assignment was already checked (when it was previously constructed/assigned). The `#[derive(Clone)]` macro could thus trivially wrap the the whole expression in `unsafe {}` when it encounters an `unsafe` field.

Is there a plausible situation where the safety of a field would rely on external factors to a struct? If not, I'll need to figure out a way to rework the RFC to make `Clone`/`Copy` acceptable.

comments.author: RalfJung
comments.body:
> it is already known that the data contained in the struct is valid

We're talking about safety invariants here, not validity invariants.
And safety invariants in Rust, in general, express ownership -- just because they hold once, doesn't mean they hold twice. A safety invariant could be "I exclusively own the right to access this region of memory": just because this is the case, doesn't mean you can make a copy and have two values where the safety invariant holds for both of them.

comments.author: jhpratt
comments.body: That is true. In any situation, I feel that there _must_ be a way to implement `Clone` and `Copy` for types containing `unsafe` fields. What form that takes I'm not certain, as the traits are (of course) safe on their own.

comments.author: CodesInChaos
comments.body:
I think requiring `unsafe impl Copy` for types with unsafe fields would be enough. You'd have to implement `Copy` and `Clone` manually, instead of deriving them (the generated code would fail, because it misses the necessary `unsafe`s). That's a bit verbose (perhaps 10 lines of code), but that's no big deal IMO.

Consider `Vec`, the fields are all `copy`able and `copying` ensures consistency between allocation size, `len` and `capacity`. But it doesn't ensure that the allocation is owned by a single vec, and hence needs a custom `Clone` and a disabled `Copy`.

comments.body:
> The problem is that (last I checked) you can't unsafe impl a safe trait. 

Yes, that rule would need a special case for `Copy` on types with unsafe fields.

> but then removing an unsafe field is a very unexpected breaking change.

Ibelieve `unsafe impl Copy` must be in the same crate as the type containing the unsafe fields, due to orphan rules. So you can adjust both at the same time, and it would not be a breaking change.

comments.author: jhpratt
comments.body: There are more traits that just `Copy` — this issue applies to _all_ traits.
